speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Give me your full name.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Lizzie Andrew Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is it Lizzie or Elizabeth?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were so christened?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was so christened.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your age, please?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Thirty-two.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your mother is not living?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did she die?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She died when I was two and a half years old.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You do not remember her, then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was your father's age?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He was seventy next month.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was his whole name?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Andrew Jackson Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And your stepmother, what is her whole name?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Abby Durfee Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long had your father been married to your stepmother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think about twenty-seven years.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much of that time have they lived in that house on Second street?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think, I am not sure, but I think about twenty years last May.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Always occupied the whole house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Somebody told me it was once fitted up for two tenements.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: When we bought it it was for two tenements, and the man we bought it of stayed there a few months until he finished his own house. After he finished his own house and moved into it there was no one else ever moved in; we always had the whole.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you any idea how much your father was worth?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you ever heard him say?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you ever formed any opinion?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know something about his real estate?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: About what?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: His real estate?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I know what real estate he owned, part of it; I don't know whether I know it all or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Tell me what you know of.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He owns two farms in Swanzey, the place on Second street and the A. J. Borden building and corner, and the land on South Main street where Mc Mannus is, and then a short time ago he bought some real estate up further south that formerly, he said, belonged to a Mr. Birch.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever deed him any property?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He gave us some years ago, Grandfather Borden's house on Ferry street, and he bought that back from us some weeks ago, I don't know just how many.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: As near as you can tell.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Well, I should say in June, but I am not sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What do you mean by bought it back?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He bought it of us, and gave us the money for it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much was it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: How much money? He gave us $5,000 for it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you pay him anything when you took a deed from him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Pay him anything? No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long ago was it you took a deed from him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: When he gave it to us?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I can't tell you; I should think five years.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you have any other business transactions with him besides that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In real estate?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or in personal property?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Never?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Never.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: No transfer of property one way or the other?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: At no time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And I understand he paid you the cash for this property?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You and Emma equally?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How many children has your father?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Only two.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Only you two?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Any others ever?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: One that died.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever know of your father making a will?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, except I heard somebody say once that there was one several years ago; that is all I ever heard.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who did you hear say so?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think it was Mr. Morse.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What Morse?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Uncle John V. Morse.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long ago?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: How long ago I heard him say it? I have not any idea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did he say about it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Nothing, except just that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: That Mr. Borden had a will.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ask your father?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he ever mention the subject of will to you?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He never told you that he had made a will, or had not?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he have a marriage settlement with your stepmother that you knew of?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I never knew of any.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you heard anything of his proposing to make a will?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know of anybody that your father was on bad terms with?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: There was a man that came there that he had trouble with, I don't know who the man was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I cannot locate the time exactly. It was within two weeks. That is I don't know the date or day of the month.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Tell all you saw and heard.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not see anything. I heard the bell ring, and father went to the door and let him in. I did not hear anything for some time, except just the voices; then I heard the man say, "I would like to have that place, I would like to have that store. Father says, "I am not willing to let your business go in there." And the man said, "I thought with your reputation for liking money, you would let your store for anything." Father said, "You are mistaken." Then they talked a while, and then their voices were louder, and I heard father order him out, and went to the front door with him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did he say?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He said that he had stayed long enough, and he would thank him to go.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he say anything about coming again?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did your father say anything about coming again, or did he?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you any idea who that was?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir. I think it was a man from out of town, because he said he was going home to see his partner.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you had any efforts made to find him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: We have had a detective; that is all I know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have not found him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Not that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You can't give us any other idea about it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Nothing but what I have told you.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Beside that do you know of anybody that your father had bad feelings toward, or who had bad feelings toward your father?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I know of one man that has not been friendly with him; they have not been friendly for years.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What relation is he to him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He is my father's brother-in-law.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your mother's brother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: My father's only sister married Mr. Harrington.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Anybody else that was on bad terms with your father, or that your father was on bad terms with?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Not that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have no reason to suppose that man you speak of a week or two ago, had ever seen your father before, or has since?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know of anybody that was on bad terms with your stepmother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or that your stepmother was on bad terms with?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had your stepmother any property?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know, only that she had half the house that belonged to her father.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: On Fourth Street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who lives in it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Her half-sister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Any other property beside that that you know of?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever know of any?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you understand that she was worth anything more than that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I never knew.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever have any trouble with your stepmother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you, within six months, had any words with her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Within a year?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Within two years?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When last that you know of?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: About five years ago.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What about?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Her stepsister, half-sister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What name?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Her name now is Mrs. George W. Whitehead.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nothing more than hard words?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, they were not hard words; it was simply a difference of opinion.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have been on pleasant terms with your stepmother since then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Cordial?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: It depends upon one's idea of cordiality, perhaps.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: According to your idea of cordiality?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: We were friendly, very friendly.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Cordial, according to your idea of cordiality?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Quite so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What do you mean by "quite so"?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Quite cordial. I do not mean the dearest of friends in the world, but very kindly feelings, and pleasant. I do not know how to answer you any better than that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not regard her as your mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Not exactly, no; although she came there when I was very young.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were your relations towards her that of daughter and mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: In some ways it was, and in some it was not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In what ways was it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I decline to answer.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because I don't know how to answer it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In what ways was it not?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not call her mother.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What name did she go by?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you begin to call her Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I should think five or six years ago.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Before that time you had called her mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What led to the change?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The affair with her stepsister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So that the affair was serious enough to have you change from calling her mother, do you mean?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not choose to call her mother.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you ever called her mother since?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, occasionally.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: To her face, I mean?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Often?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Seldom?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Seldom.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your usual address was Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did your sister Emma call her mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She always called her Abby from the time she came into the family.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is your sister Emma older than you?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is her age?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She is ten years older than I am. She was somewhere about fourteen when she came there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was your stepmother's age?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know. I asked her sister Saturday, and she said sixty-four. I told them sixty-seven; I did not know. I told as nearly as I knew. I did not know there was so much difference between she and father.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why did you leave off calling her mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because I wanted to.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is that all the reason you have to give me?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have not any other answer.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Can't you give me any better reason than that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have not any reason to give, except that I did not want to.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In what other respect were the relations between you and her not that of mother and daughter, besides not calling her mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know that any of the relations were changed. I had never been to her as a mother in many things. I always went to my sister, because she was older and had the care of me after my mother died.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In what respects were the relations between you and her that of mother and daughter?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: That is the same question you asked before; I can't answer you any better now than I did before.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not say before you could not answer, but that you declined to answer.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I decline to answer because I do not know what to say.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is the only reason?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You called your father father?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Always.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were your father and mother happily united?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Why, I don't know but that they were.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why do you hesitate?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because I don't know but that they were, and I am telling the truth as nearly as I know it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you mean me to understand that they were happy entirely, or not?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: So far as I know they were.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why did you hesitate then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because I did not know how to answer you any better than what came into my mind. I was trying to think if I was telling it as I should; that is all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you have any difficulty in telling it as you should, any difficulty in answering my questions?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Some of your questions I have difficulty in answering, because I don't know just how you mean them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever know of any difficulty between her and your father?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he seem to be affectionate?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: As man and woman who are married ought to be?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: So far as I have ever had any chance of judging.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: They were?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What dress did you wear the day they were killed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had on a navy blue, sort of a bengaline or India silk skirt, with a navy blue blouse. In the afternoon they thought I had better change it. I put on a pink wrapper.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you change your clothing before the afternoon?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dressed in the morning, as you have described, and kept that clothing on until afternoon?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did Morse come there first, I don't mean this visit, I mean as a visitor, John V. Morse?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Do you mean this day that he came and stayed all night?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: No. Was this visit his first to your house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He has been in the east a year or more.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Since he has been in the east has he been in the habit of coming to your house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes; came in any time he wanted to.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Before that had he been at your house, before he came east?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, he has been here, if you remember the winter that the river was frozen over and they went across, he was here that winter, some 14 years ago, was it not?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I am not answering questions, but asking them.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't remember the date. He was here that winter.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Has he been here since?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He has been here once since; I don't know whether he has or not since.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How many times this last year has he been at your house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: None at all to speak of; nothing more than a night or two at a time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How often did he come to spend a night or two?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Really I don't know; I am away so much myself.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your last answer is that you don't know how much he had been here, because you had been away yourself so much?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is true the last year, or since he has been east?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have not been away the last year so much, but other times I have been away when he has been here.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do I understand you to say that his last visit before this one was 14 years ago?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, he has been here once between the two.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long did he stay then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long ago was that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Give me your best remembrance.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Five or six years, perhaps six.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long has he been east this time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think over a year; I am not sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: During the last year how much of the time has he been at your house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Very little that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your answer to that question before was, I don't know because I have been away so much myself.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not mean I had been away very much myself the last year.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much have you been away the last year?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have been away a great deal in the daytime, occasionally at night.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where in the daytime, any particular place?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, around town.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you go off nights, where?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Never unless I have been off on a visit?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was the last time when you have been away for more than a night or two before this affair?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't think I have been away to stay more than a night or two since I came from abroad, except about three or four weeks ago I was in New Bedford for three or four days.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where at New Bedford?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: At 20 Madison street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long ago were you abroad?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was abroad in 1890.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did he come to the house the last time before your father and mother were killed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He stayed there all night Wednesday night.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: My question is when he came there.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know; I was not at home when he came; I was out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you first see him there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not see him at all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did you know he was there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I heard his voice.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see him Wednesday evening?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not; I was out Wednesday evening.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see him Thursday morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not; he was out when I came down stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was the first time you saw him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Thursday noon.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had never seen him before that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where were you Wednesday evening?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I spent the evening with Miss Russell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: As near as you can remember, when did you return?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: About nine o'clock at night.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The family had then retired?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know whether they had or not. I went right to my room; I don't remember.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not look to see?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which door did you come in at?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The front door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you lock it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: For the night?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And went right up stairs to your room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was it that you heard the voice of Mr. Morse?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I heard him down there about supper time. No, it was earlier than that. I heard him down there somewhere about three o'clock, I think. I was in my room Wednesday, not feeling well, all day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you eat supper at home Wednesday night?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was at home; I did not eat any supper, because I did not feel able to eat supper; I had been sick.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not come down to supper?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear him eating supper?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir. I did not know whether he was there or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You heard him in the afternoon?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear him go away?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not go down to see him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was you in bed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, I was on the lounge.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why did you not go down?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not care to go down, and I was not feeling well, and kept my room all day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You felt better in the evening?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Not very much better. I thought I would go out, and see if the air would make me feel any better.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you came back at nine o'clock, you did not look in to see if the family were up?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why not?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I very rarely do when I come in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You go right to your room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you have a night key?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did you know it was right to lock the front door?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: That was always my business.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How many locks did you fasten?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The spring locks itself, and there is a key to turn, and you manipulate the bolt.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You manipulated all those?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I used them all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then you went to bed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, directly.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you got up the next morning, did you see Mr. Morse?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had the family breakfasted when you came down?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did you come down stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: As near as I can remember, it was a few minutes before nine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who did you find down stairs when you came down?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Maggie and Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you inquire for Mr. Morse?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you suppose he had gone?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not know whether he had or not; he was not there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your father was there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then you found him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you speak to either your father or Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I spoke to them all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: About Mr. Morse?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not mention him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did not inquire anything about him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long before that time had he been at the house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: As near as you can tell?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know. He was there in June sometime, I don't know whether he was there after that or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why did you not go to Marion with the party that went?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because they went sooner than I could, and I was going Monday.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why did they go sooner than you could; what was there to keep you?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had taken the secretaryship and treasurer of our C. E. society, had the charge, and the roll call was the first Sunday in August, and I felt I must be there and attend to that part of the business.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was your sister Emma that day?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: What day?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The day your father and Mrs. Borden were killed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She had been in Fairhaven.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you written to her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was the last time you wrote to her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Thursday morning, and my father mailed the letter for me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she get it at Fairhaven?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, it was sent back. She did not get it at Fairhaven, for we telegraphed for her, and she got home here Thursday afternoon, and the letter was sent back to this post office.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long had she been in Fairhaven?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Just two weeks to a day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not visit in Fairhaven?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had there been anybody else around the house that week, or premises?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No one that I know of, except the man that called to see him on this business about the store.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that that week?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I misunderstood you probably, I thought you said a week or two before.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, I said that week. There was a man came the week before and gave up some keys, and I took them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember of anybody else being then around the premises that week?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Nobody that I know of or saw.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nobody at work there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nobody doing any chores there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, not that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nobody had access to the house, so far as you know, during that time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I ask you once more how it happened that, knowing Mr. Morse was at your house, you did not step in and greet him before you retired?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have no reason, except that I was not feeling well Wednesday, and so did not come down.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: No, you were down. When you came in from out.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Do you mean Wednesday night?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because I hardly ever do go in. I generally went right up to my room, and I did that night.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Could you then get to your room from the back hall?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: From the back stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Why not? What would hinder?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Father's bedroom door was kept locked, and his door into my room was locked and hooked too. I think, and I had no keys.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was the custom of the establishment?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: It had always been so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was so Wednesday, and so Thursday?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: It was so Wednesday, but Thursday they broke the door open.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was after the crowd came; before the crowd came?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: It was so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: There was no access, except one had a key, and one would have to have two keys?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: They would have to have two keys if they went up the back way to get into my room. If they were in my room, they would have to have a key to get into his room, and another to get into the back stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did Mr. Morse sleep?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: In the next room over the parlor in front of the stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Right up the same stairs that your room was?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How far from your room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: A door opened into it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The two rooms connected directly?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: By one door, that is all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not through the hall?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was the door locked?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: It has been locked and bolted, and a large writing desk in my room kept up against it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then it was not a practical opening?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How otherwise do you get from your room to the next room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have to go into the front hall.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How far apart are the two doors?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Very near, I don't think more than so far (measuring.)

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it your habit when you were in your room to keep your door shut?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That time, that Wednesday afternoon?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: My door was open part of the time, and part of the time I tried to get a nap and their voices annoyed me, and I closed it. I kept it open in summer more or less, and closed in winter.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then, unless for some special reason, you kept your door open in the summer?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir, if it was a warm day. If it was a cool day, I should have closed it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was your father when you came down Thursday morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Sitting in the sitting room in his large chair, reading the Providence Journal.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was your mother? Do you prefer me to call her Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had as soon you called her mother. She was in the dining room with a feather duster dusting.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When she dusted did she wear something over her head?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Sometimes when she swept, but not when dusting.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was Maggie?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Just come in the back door with the long pole, brush, and put the brush on the handle, and getting her pail of water; she was going to wash the windows around the house. She said Mrs. Borden wanted her to.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you get your breakfast that morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not eat any breakfast; I did not feel as though I wanted any.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you get any breakfast that morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know whether I ate half a banana; I don't think I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You drank no tea or coffee that morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And ate no cookies?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know whether I did or not. We had some molasses cookies; I don't know whether I ate any that morning or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were the breakfast things put away when you got down?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Everything except the coffee pot; I am not sure whether that was on the stove or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You said nothing about Mr. Morse to your father or mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the next thing that happened after you got down?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Maggie went out of doors to wash the windows and father came out into the kitchen and said he did not know whether he would go down to the post office or not. And then I sprinkled some handkerchiefs to iron.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Tell me again what time you came down stairs.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: It was a little before nine, I should say about quarter; I don't know sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did your father go down town?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He went down later.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did he start away?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What were you doing when he started away?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was in the dining room I think; yes, I had just commenced, I think, to iron.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It may seem a foolish question. How much of an ironing did you have?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I only had about eight or ten of my best handkerchiefs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you let your father out?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir; he went out himself.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you fasten the door after him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Maggie?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know. When she went up stairs she always locked the door; she had charge of the back door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she go out after a brush before your father went away?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you say anything to Maggie?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you say anything about washing the windows?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you speak to her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think I told her I did not want any breakfast.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You do not remember of talking about washing the windows?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't remember whether I did or not; I don't remember it. Yes, I remember; yes, I asked her to shut the parlor blinds when she got through, because the sun was so hot.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: About what time do you think your father went down town?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know; it must have been after nine o'clock. I don't know what time it was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You think at that time you had begun to iron your handkerchiefs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long a job was that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not finish them; my flats were not hot enough.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long a job would it have been if the flats had been right?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: If they had been hot, not more than 20 minutes, perhaps.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long did you work on the job?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long was your father gone?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where were you when he returned?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was down in the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What doing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Reading an old magazine that had been left in the cupboard, an old Harper's Magazine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you got through ironing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you stopped ironing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Stopped for the flats.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you waiting for them to be hot?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was there a fire in the stove?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When your father went away, you were ironing then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had not commenced, but I was getting the little ironing board and the flannel.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Are you sure you were in the kitchen when your father returned?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I am not sure whether I was there or in the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go back to your room before your father returned?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think I did carry up some clean clothes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you stay there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you spend any time up the front stairs before your father returned?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or after he returned?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, sir. I did stay in my room long enough when I went up to sew a little piece of tape on a garment.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that the time when your father came home?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He came home after I came down stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were not up stairs when he came home?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was not up stairs when he came home; no, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was Maggie doing when your father came home?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know whether she was there or whether she had gone up stairs; I can't remember.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who let your father in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think he came to the front door and rang the bell, and I think Maggie let him in, and he said he had forgotten his key; so I think she must have been down stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: His key would have done him no good if the locks were left as you left them?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: But they were always unbolted in the morning.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who unbolted them that morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't think they had been unbolted; Maggie can tell you.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: If he had not forgotten his key it would have been no good?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, he had his key and could not get in. I understood Maggie to say he said he had forgotten his key.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not hear him say anything about it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I heard his voice, but I don't know what he said.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I understood you to say he said he had forgotten his key?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, it was Maggie said he said he had forgotten the key.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was Maggie when the bell rang?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where were you when the bell rang?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think in my room up stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then you were up stairs when your father came home?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know sure, but I think I was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What were you doing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: As I say, I took up these clean clothes, and stopped and basted a little piece of tape on a garment.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you come down before your father was let in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was on the stairs coming down when she let him in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then you were up stairs when your father came to the house on his return?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think I was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long had you been there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had only been upstairs just long enough to take the clothes up and baste the little loop on the sleeve. I don't think I had been up there over five minutes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was Maggie still engaged in washing windows when your father got back?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You remember, Miss Borden, I will call your attention to it so as to see if I have any misunderstanding, not for the purpose of confusing you; you remember that you told me several times that you were down stairs, and not up stairs when your father came home? You have forgotten, perhaps?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know what I have said. I have answered so many questions and I am so confused I don't know one thing from another. I am telling you just as nearly as I know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Calling your attention to what you said about that a few minutes ago, and now again to the circumstance you have said you were up stairs when the bell rang, and were on the stairs when Maggie let your father in; which now is your recollection of the true statement of the matter, that you were down stairs when the bell rang and your father came?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think I was down stairs in the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And then you were not up stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think I was not; because I went up almost immediately, as soon as I went down, and then came down again and stayed down.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What had you in your mind when you said you were on the stairs as Maggie let your father in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The other day somebody came there and she let them in and I was on the stairs; I don't know whether the morning before or when it was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You understood I was asking you exactly and explicitly about this fatal day?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I now call your attention to the fact that you had specifically told me you had gone up stairs, and had been there about five minutes when the bell rang, and were on your way down, and were on the stairs when Maggie let your father in that day.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, I said that, and then I said I did not know whether I was on the stairs or in the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Now how will you have it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think, as nearly as I know, I think I was in the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long was your father gone?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know, sir; not very long.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: An hour?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I should not think so.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I sprinkled my handkerchiefs, and got my ironing board and took them in the dining room. I took the ironing board in the dining room and left the handkerchiefs in the kitchen on the table and whether I ate any cookies or not I don't remember. Then I sat down looking at the magazine, waiting for the flats to heat. Then I went in the sitting room and got the Providence Journal, and took that into the kitchen. I don't recollect of doing anything else.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which did you read first, the Journal or the magazine?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The magazine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You told me you were reading the magazine when your father came back?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I said in the kitchen, yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that so?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, I took the Journal out to read, and had not read it. I had it near me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You said a minute or two ago you read the magazine awhile, and then went and got the Journal and took it out to read?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did, but I did not read it; I tried my flats then.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And went back to reading the magazine?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I took the magazine up again, yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you last see your mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not see her after when I went down in the morning and she was dusting the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you or she go then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know where she went. I know where I was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you or she leave the dining room first?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think I did. I left her in the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You never saw her or heard her afterwards?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she say anything about making the bed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She said she had been up and made the bed up fresh, and had dusted the room and left it all in order. She was going to put some fresh pillow slips on the small pillows at the foot of the bed, and was going to close the room, because she was going to have company Monday and she wanted everything in order.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long would it take to put on the pillow slips?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: About two minutes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long to do the rest of the things?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She had done that when I came down.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: All that was left was what?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: To put on the pillow slips.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Can you give me any suggestion as to what occupied her when she was up there, when she was struck dead?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know of anything except she had some cotton cloth pillow cases up there, and she said she was going to commence to work on them. That is all I know. And the sewing machine was up there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Whereabouts was the sewing machine?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: In the comer between the north and west side.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear the sewing machine going?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see anything to indicate that the sewing machine had been used that morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had not. I did not go in there until after everybody had been in there, and the room had been overhauled.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: If she had remained down stairs, you would undoubtedly have seen her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: If she had remained down stairs, I should have; if she had remained in her room, I should not have. If she had remained down stairs, I should have. If she had gone to her room, I should not have.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Over the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: To get to that room she would have to go through the kitchen?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: To get up the back stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is the way she was in the habit of going?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir, because the other doors were locked.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: If she had remained down stairs, or had gone to her own room, you undoubtedly would have seen her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I should have seen her if she had stayed down stairs; if she had gone to her room, I would not have seen her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She was found a little after 11 in the spare room, if she had gone to her own room she must have gone through the kitchen and up the back stairs, and subsequently have gone down and gone back again?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you any reason to suppose you would not have seen her if she had spent any portion of the time in her own room, or down stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: There is no reason why I should not have seen her if she had been down there, except when I first came down stairs, for two or three minutes I went down cellar to the water closet.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After that you were where you practically commanded the view of the first story the rest of the time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you went up stairs for a short time, as you say you did, you then went in sight of the sewing machine?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, I did not see the sewing machine, because she had shut that room up.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What do you mean?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I mean the door was closed. She said she wanted it kept closed to keep the dust and everything out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it a room with a window?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: It has three windows.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A large room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The size of the parlor; a pretty fair sized room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It is the guest room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, the spare room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where the sewing machine was was the guest room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I ask again, perhaps you have answered all you care to, what explanation can you give, can you suggest, as to what she was doing from the time she said she had got the work all done in the spare room until 11 o'clock?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I suppose she went up and made her own bed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That would be in the back part?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She would have to go by you twice to do that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Unless she went when I was in my room that few minutes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That would not be time enough for her to go and make her own bed and come back again?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Sometimes she stayed up longer and sometimes shorter; I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Otherwise than that, she would have to go in your sight?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I should have to have seen her once; I don't know that I need to have seen her more than once.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see her at all?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, not after the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What explanation can you suggest as to the whereabouts of your mother from the time you saw her in the dining room, and she said her work in the spare room was all done, until 11 o'clock?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know. I think she went back into the spare room, and whether she came back again or not I don't know; that has always been a mystery.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Can you think of anything she could be doing in the spare room?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir. I know what she used to do sometimes. She kept her best cape she wore on the street in there, and she used occasionally to go up there to get it and to take it into her room. She kept a great deal in the guest room drawers; she used to go up there and get things and put things; she used those drawers for her own use.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That connects her with her own room again, to reach which she had to go down stairs and come up again?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Assuming that she did not go into her own room, I understand you to say she could not have gone to her own room without your seeing her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She could while I was down cellar.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You went down immediately you came down, within a few minutes, and you did not see her when you came back?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After that time she must have remained in the guest chamber?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So far as you can judge?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: So far as I can judge she might have been out of the house, or in the house.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you any knowledge of her going out of the house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you any knowledge of her going out of the house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She told me she had had a note, somebody was sick, and said "I am going to get the dinner on the way," and asked me what I wanted for dinner.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you tell her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, I told her I did not want anything.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then why did you not suppose she had gone?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I supposed she had gone.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear her come back?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not hear her go or come back, but I supposed she went.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you found your father dead you supposed your mother had gone?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not know. I said to the people who came in "I don't know whether Mrs. Borden is out or in; I wish you would see if she is in her room."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You supposed she was out at the time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I understood so; I did not suppose anything about it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she tell you where she was going?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she tell you who the note was from?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever see the note?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know where it is now?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She said she was going out that morning?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I shall have to ask you once more about that morning. Do you know what the family ate for breakfast?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had the breakfast all been cleared away when you got down?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I want you to tell me just where you found the people when you got down that you did find there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I found Mrs. Borden in the dining room. I found my father in the sitting room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And Maggie?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Maggie was coming in the back door with her pail and brush.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Tell me what talk you had with your mother at that time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She asked me how I felt. I said I felt better than I did Tuesday, but I did not want any breakfast. She asked me what I wanted for dinner. I told her nothing. I told her I did not want anything. She said she was going out, and would get the dinner. That is the last I saw her, or said anything to her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you go then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Into the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Down cellar.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Gone perhaps five minutes?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Perhaps. Not more than that; possibly a little bit more.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you came back did you see your mother?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not; I supposed she had gone out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She did not tell you where she was going?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you came back was your father there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was he doing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Reading the paper.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you eat any breakfast?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, I don't remember whether I ate a molasses cookie or not. I did not eat any regularly prepared breakfast.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it usual for your mother to go out?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir, she went out every morning nearly, and did the marketing.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it usual for her to be gone away from dinner?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir, sometimes, not very often.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How often, say?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Oh, I should not think more than, well I don't know, more than once in three months, perhaps.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Now I call your attention to the fact that twice yesterday you told me, with some explicitness, that when your father came in, you were just coming down stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No, I did not, I beg your pardon.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That you were on the stairs at the time your father was let in, you said with some explicitness. Do you now say you did not say so?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I said I thought first I was on the stairs; then I remembered I was in the kitchen when he came in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: First you thought you were in the kitchen; afterwards you remembered you were on the stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I said I thought I was on the stairs; then I said I knew I was in the kitchen. I still say that now. I was in the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go into the front part of the house after your father came in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: After he came in from down street I was in the sitting room with him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go into the front hall afterwards?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: At no time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Excepting the two or three minutes you were down cellar, were you away from the house until your father came in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were always in the kitchen or dining room, excepting when you went up stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I went up stairs before he went out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You mean you went up there to sew a button on?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I basted a piece of tape on.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember you did not say that yesterday?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't think you asked me. I told you yesterday I went up stairs directly after I came up from down cellar, with the clean clothes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You now say after your father went out, you did not go up stairs at all?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When Maggie came in there washing the windows, you did not appear from the front part of the house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When your father was let in, you did not appear from up stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, I was in the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is so?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir, to the best of my knowledge.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After your father went out, you remained there either in the kitchen or dining room all the time?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I went in the sitting room long enough to direct some paper wrappers.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: One of the three rooms?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So it would have been extremely difficult for anybody to have gone through the kitchen and dining room and front hall, without your seeing them?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: They could have gone from the kitchen into the sitting room while I was in the dining room, if there was anybody to go.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then into the front hall?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were in the dining room ironing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir, part of the time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were in all of the three rooms?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A large portion of that time, the girl was out of doors?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know where she was, I did not see her. I supposed she was out of doors, as she had the pail and brush.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You knew she was washing windows?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: She told me she was going to. I did not see her do it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: For a large portion of the time you did not see the girl?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So far as you know you were alone in the lower part of the house, a large portion of the time, after your father went away, and before he came back?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: My father did not go away I think until somewhere about 10, as near as I can remember; he was with me down stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A large portion of the time after your father went away, and before he came back, so far as you know, you were alone in the house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Maggie had come in and gone up stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After he went out, and before he came back; a large portion of the time after your father went out, and before he came back, so far as you know, you were the only person in the house?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: So far as I know, I was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And during that time, so far as you know, the front door was locked?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: So far as I know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And never was unlocked at all?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't think it was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Even after your father came home, it was locked up again?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know whether she locked it up again after that or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It locks itself?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The spring lock opens.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It fastens it so it cannot be opened from the outside?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Sometimes you can press it open.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you any reason to suppose the spring lock was left so it could be pressed open from the outside?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have no reason to suppose so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nothing about the lock was changed before the people came?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Nothing that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What were you doing in the kitchen when your father came home?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think I was eating a pear when he came in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What had you been doing before that?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Been reading a magazine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you making preparations to iron again?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had sprinkled my clothes, and was waiting for the flat. I sprinkled the clothes before he went out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you built up the fire again?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I put in a stick of wood. There was a few sparks. I put in a stick of wood to try to heat the flat.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had then started the fire?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The fire was burning when he came in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, but it was smoldering and smoking as though it would come up.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did it come up after he came in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you do any more ironing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not. I went in with him, and did not finish.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not iron any more after your father came in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was the ironing board put away?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir, it was on the dining room table.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was it put away?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know. Somebody put it away after the affair happened.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not put it away?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it on the dining room table when you found your father killed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I suppose so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had not put it away then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I had not touched it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How soon after your father came in, before Maggie went up stairs?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know. I did not see her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see her after your father came in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Not after she let him in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long was your father in the house before you found him killed?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know exactly, because I went out to the barn. I don't know what time he came home. I don't think he had been home more than fifteen or twenty minutes; I am not sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you went out to the barn, where did you leave your father?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: He had laid down on the sitting room lounge, taken off his shoes, and put on his slippers, and taken off his coat and put on the reefer. I asked him if he wanted the window left that way.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you leave him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: On the sofa.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was he asleep?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was he reading?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the last thing you said to him?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I asked him if he wanted the window left that way. Then I went into the kitchen, and from there to the barn.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Whereabouts in the barn did you go?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Up stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: To the second story of the barn?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long did you remain there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know, fifteen or twenty minutes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What doing?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Trying to find lead for a sinker.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What made you think there would be lead for a sinker up there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because there was some there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was there not some by the door?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Some pieces of lead by the open door, but there was a box full of old things up stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you bring any sinker back from the barn?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I found no sinker.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you bring any sinker back from the barn?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Nothing but a piece of a chip I picked up on the floor.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was that box you say was up stairs, containing lead?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: There was a kind of a work bench.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is it there now?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long since have you seen it there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I have not been out there since that day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you been in the barn before?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: That day, no sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long since you had been in the barn before?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't think I had been into it, I don't know as I had in three months.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you went out did you unfasten the screen door?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I unhooked it to get out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was hooked until you went out?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It had been left hooked by Bridget, if she was the last one in?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I suppose so; I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know when she did get through washing the outside?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know she washed the windows inside?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see her washing the windows inside?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You don't know whether she washed the dining room and sitting room windows inside?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I did not see her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: If she did would you not have seen her?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know. She might be in one room and I in another.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you think she might have gone to work and washed all the windows in the dining room and sitting room and you not know it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know, I am sure, whether I should or not. I might have seen her, and not know it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Miss Borden, I am trying in good faith to get all the doings that morning of yourself and Miss Sullivan, and I have not succeeded in doing it. Do you desire to give me any information or not?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know it- I don't know what your name is.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It is certain beyond reasonable doubt she was engaged in washing the windows in the dining room or sitting room when your father came home. Do you mean to say you know nothing of either of those operations?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I knew she washed the windows outside; that is, she told me so. She did not wash the windows in the kitchen, because I was in the kitchen most of the time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The dining room and sitting room, I said.

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It is reasonably certain she washed the windows in the dining room and sitting room inside while your father was out, and was engaged in that operation when your father came home; do you mean to say you know nothing of it?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't know whether she washed the windows in the sitting room and dining room or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Can you give me any information how it happened at that particular time you should go into the chamber of the barn to find a sinker to go to Marion with to fish the next Monday?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I was going to finish my ironing; my flats were not hot; I said to myself "I will go and try and find that sinker; perhaps by the time I get back the flats will be hot." That is the only reason.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long had you been reading an old magazine before you went to the barn at all?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Perhaps half an hour.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you got a fish line?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Not here; we had some at the farm.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you got a fish hook?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: No sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you got any apparatus for fishing at all?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, over there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you any sinkers over there?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I think there were some. It is so long since I have been there; I think there were some.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had no reason to suppose you were lacking sinkers?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't think there were any on my lines.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where were your lines?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: My fish lines were at the farm here.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What made you think there were no sinkers at the farm on your lines?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Because some time ago when I was there I had none.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long since you used the fish lines?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Five years, perhaps.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You left them at the farm then?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And you have not seen them since?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: Yes, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It occurred to you after your father came in it would be a good time to go to the barn after sinkers, and you had no reason to suppose there was not abundance of sinkers at the farm and abundance of lines?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: The last time I was there there were some lines.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you not say before you presumed there were sinkers at the farm?

speaker: Lizzie Borden
testimony: I don't think I said so.
