speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you done so in six months?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: O, yes several times.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You say that the day before the tragedy, you came about what time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I left New Bedford on the 12.35 train. I suppose I got to his house about half past one.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Eat dinner there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir, they had been to dinner.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you written you were coming?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you in the habit, since this year and a half of being here, of writing to any of them?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I wrote to Mr. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you written to either of your neices?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Emma has to me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Never to Lizzie?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Lizzie has not written you, nor you to her?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have no letters from Lizzie in your possession?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You do not think you had written announcing your visit at this time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont think I did. Let me see, let me tell it as I can think of it. Mr. Borden, when I was over here sometime in July, that I speak of, wanted to know if I knew of a man he could get on his farm, to take charge of it, I told him I did not know, I would see. When I got back I wrote him I knew of a man I thought would suit him, I would send him over. He wrote back to me he had rather I would wait until I saw him. I have his letter in my pocket, if you want to see it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the date of that letter? You may refresh your memory. If you have no objections, I will see it.

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Witness produces the letter dated July 25, 1892.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you any objection to me keeping this?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir, I would not like it lost, because it was the last one I ever had from him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That, then, was the last correspondence before you came over?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: That is the last.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not write him you were coming?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You came partially in pursuance of that request?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that about ten days before you came?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So they were not expecting you that particular day, but were looking for you at any time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who did you see at the house when you got there that noon?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Mr. Borden, that is Andrew, and his wife.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And the servant girl?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes, I saw her first.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Emma was away?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Now then, tell what happened that day.

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Well, I went in there, and the first thing, I asked the girl, she was the first person I saw when I got there, I asked if Mr. Borden was at home, or Andrew, I dont know which. She said he was on the lounge.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Go on.

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I went in, and he got up. The lounge was on this side of the room; him and Mrs. Borden sat there and chatted. He asked if I had been to dinner. I told him I had not, but was not hungry at all. Mrs. Borden said "we have just had dinner, a little while ago, it is all warm, I will put it on." She did, in the dining room. I sat down and ate, and we went back in the sitting room and chatted again until between three and four. I was going to Swansea. I came over to Kirby's stable, hired a horse and buggy and went over to Swansea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: With Mr. Borden?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir. I asked him to go. He said he did not feel able to; they were indisposed, all of them that day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And the daughter?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes, Mrs. Borden told me they had all been sick.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who did you see at the farm?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: A man by the name of Frank.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The Swede?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No, an American, Frank Eddy. I can think, if you give me a little time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Anybodyelse?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I saw what I supposed to be his wife; I never was acquainted with her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Any other farm hands?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Stay to supper over there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir. I ate supper at William Vinnicum's, a little beyond there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In Warren?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No, in Swansea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Got back home about what time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I got back to the house probably quarter to nine, not far from that, after dark.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you got there, who did you find at home then?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think the girl was there, the servant, and Mr. and Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And Lizzie?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I know they were there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you make any inquires about Lizzie?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nothing was said about her?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she come in while you were there? Did you hear or see her come in?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I heard the front door shut. The girls usually come in, and go up stairs when they come in with their clothes on in that way. I heard her come in, I supposed it was her; Emma was not at home.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did you retire that night?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Mr. and Mrs. Borden and me sat there and talked half an hour probably, and Mrs. Borden retired. Mr. Borden and me probably sat there until about ten o'clock. He says "John, is it not about time we went to bed"? I says "it is about ten, isnt it?" I think it is.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So you both went to bed together?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No, he went up to [his room, using the back staircase and I went up to the guest room using the front staircase].

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: At the same time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You slept in the same room where this woman was found dead the next day?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In the morning, what time did you arise?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think I got up about six o'clock.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was you the first one up?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think so, I did not see anybody in the room when I got up.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was the servant up?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think not, she was not down stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who was the first one you did see?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Mr. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He got up before the servant girl?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I saw him first.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did he come out?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think he had been up some half hour when he came down, very near to it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did he appear to be that morning in his health?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: He said he felt some better.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you happen to know of his taking some medicine?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I asked him to go over to Swansea. He said he did not feelable to. I says "I will wait until morning, if you will go". He said no he had been taking some medicine, and did not think he would go.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he say what it was?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Physic, I supposed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He did not say what it was?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see Mrs. Borden?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: She came down soon after.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did she appear to be?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: She said she felt real well that day, that is, to what she had done.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did you have breakfast?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: A little after seven I think.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Are you pretty sure about the time of breakfast?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont think it could have been more than twenty minutes past seven; I judge abut that time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Can you remember what you had for breakfast?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont know as I do know. I know what we had the day before for dinner.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did they have the day before for dinner?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Some veal, sort of a soup like.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That would not warm over for breakfast then?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I could not tell what they had.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did they have some kind of meat?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think some kind.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember whether they had some sort of fruit, apples, pears, or bananas?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: There was bananas on the table.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Further than that, you cannot remember?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did they all eat pretty heartily?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Not very.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: They both ate, both Mr. and Mrs. Borden?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did they eat the fruit?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont think they did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you leave the house?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Well, about nine, but I should think maybe quarter or twenty minutes of nine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was going on in the intervening time, before you went?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: We went out in the sitting room from the dining room, and Mr. and Mrs. Borden and I talked a little while; then she went to dusting around, doing her little chores. Then Mr. Borden and I talked about some cattle I had; and then I went away.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you say where you was going?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir. He was telling the night before, up at Mr. Emery's I had a nephew and neice from the West, and he told me where they lived, and wanted me to go and see them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he tell you where they lived?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir, 4 Weybosset street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you tell him you were going?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir. As I went out the door, he says "John, come back to dinner with us". That is that last he spoke to me. I said I would. I came to the Post Office and got a car.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you came from New Bedford to Fall River did you have any set time to go back?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Not particular, no. I told Mr. Davis I would try to get back the next day. He says you will be gone two days, I guess, I will give you that. I could have gone back the night before at six o'clock or half past.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You told Mr. Borden you would come back to dinner, when he asked you?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had Lizzie got up when you went away?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I had not seen her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you went away was Mrs. Borden down stairs?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont think she was when I went away, she was there a few minutes before. I dont think I saw her when I came out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long before you went away, did you see her?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think not more than ten minutes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She had been about most of the time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which door did you go out of?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: The back door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it fastened when you went out?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes, and Mr. Borden opened it and let me out, and hooked it himself.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After you got out?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it the habit to keep that hooked?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Always.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And the front door?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Always keep it fastened; they have been very cautious, always have been, about the doors.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you take notice what the servant girl was doing when you went out?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont know. I heard Mrs. Borden tell her at breakfast time, if it was so she could, she would like for her to wash the windows.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear Mrs. Borden say anything about going out that morning?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know about her receiving any note?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Only by hear-say since.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was that?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I heard there was a note came, and that somebody was sick.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who told you that, anybody of the family?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not hear anything of it at the time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did Lizzie first tell you that?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think in the afternoon.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That same afternoon?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She did not say who the note came from?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No, she did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nor where she was to go?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: But she had not gone when you came away?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How came Mrs. Borden to speak to the servant girl at the breakfast table?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont know, I suppose it came into her mind.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The servant girl did not eat with you?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was she in the room there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: In and out of the room, the door was open.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Into the kitchen. Had she begun to wash the windows when you went away?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I could not tell you that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see her when you went out through the kitchen?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did she appear to be doing then?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I did not notice.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Wont you detail your movements after you left the house?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: That day?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Yes.

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I left there 15 or 20 minutes of nine, came down to the Post Office, wrote a postal, and went up Bedford street to Third street, and went from there to Pleasant street, and up to Weybosset street, No. 4, Dan Emery's.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Go on. How long did you stay there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think I stayed there until about twenty minutes past eleven, maybe a little later.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You got there about ten?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: It must have been earlier.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see the relatives you went there to see?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I saw one; the young man was out, I did not see him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the young woman's name?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Annie Morse, she was indisposed while I was there, she was on the lounge part of the time. She is my brother's daughter.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had not seen her for a good while?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: A great many years.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she come from the same part of the West you lived?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: She belonged up in Minnesota. I went there first.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The first you heard of her being there was from Mr. Borden?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No, I was at her Grandmother's, they told me she was there, and had gone to Providence with one of her cousins. When I got off the cars, they got on. I just barely saw her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you ever been to Emery's before?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Not this time, I had six or seven years before when they lived on North Main street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What occasioned you to leave at twenty minutes past eleven?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Nothing, I thought it was about time to be going down.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did the Borden family dine?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Twelve, about.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did they ask you to stay to dinner?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir. I told them I had another engagement.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The engagement you had was that engagement to dine with your brother?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you walk up?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No, came on the street car.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did you get to the Borden house?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think it must be pretty well towards twelve, within 15 or 20 minutes of it, I dont know but it is quite that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you get off the car?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: At Second street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And walked up Second street?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was the first you heard that Mr. Borden was killed?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: When I went into the door. I went around, before I went into the house, to a pear tree to get a couple of pears. When I came back, the servant girl met me at the door, and asked if I had heard the news. I said no. She said Mr. and Mrs. Borden were both murdered. A man named Sawyer stood there at the time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was anybody else in the house then?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: A man named Bowen, I think several policemen, I dont know. There was so much excitement, I know there was several women there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had Dr. Dolan, this man here, come, the Medical Examiner?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I could not tell you.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: They had found Mrs. Borden at that time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes, or she could not have told me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you see Lizzie when you got in there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think in the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who was she with?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: That I could not tell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How many people were in the house, do you think, when you got there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont know, I think six or seven or eight. There was quite a number there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were there any people out on the street?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I did not see them when I went in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see any excitement in the yard or ot the street?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Nothing to attract my attention at all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The car you came down on , came straight down Pleasant street from where you got on?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How far is it up there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I should think a good mile.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was not there a good many people in the street about that time, when you came down?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I did not see anything to attract my attention at all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you not see anything to attract your attention to the house when you went by?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir, saw nothing to attract my attention at all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nobody in the house at all?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: All I could see in was in the screen door, I went right by, the same as I would on any occasion.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you get your pears?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then started for the house?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you stop to eat the pears?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think I was eating one when I went to the door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Tell me please, as near as you can, who you saw there that you can name, when you got into the house?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Why, the first two that were right together, were a man named Sawyer, and this servant girl, Bridget something.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Maggie we will call her.

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes. The next I recognized was Dr. Bowen. There was such an excitement, and I was nerved up, I saw a number. I noticed Dr. Bowen right away.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see anybody else you did know besides those three and Lizzie?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes, Lizzie. I dont know of anyonelse, I cant say now.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How many people altogether in the house?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Seven or eight I think I saw, I dont think more than that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What officers were there?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I could not tell you.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Some officers have talked with you since that time; were any of the officers that have talked with you there then?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I could not tell you, I did not notice.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know anything about the condition of Mr. Borden's property?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Nothing particular.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had he ever talked with you about his property?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Some, occasionally.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Ever give you an idea of how much he was worth?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he ever talk with you about a will?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Yes Sir, he has.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was the last time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Somewhere within a year.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you were there at the house?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: No Sir, I think we were outside at the time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the talk?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: He said he thought he should make some bequests outside to charitable purposes. He did not say any more either one way or the other.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he say he had made a will?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: He did not say .

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He did not say whether he had or not?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Whether he had or had not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he talk as though he was intending to make a will?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I judged from that that he was intending to, I drew my conclusions that he had not, but was thinking of it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he mention the bequests outside he thought he should make?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: He did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How came he to be speaking about it?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Common conversation, I suppose, same as about his land. Before he bought the Birch land, I was down there with him. He says lets go up Main street. We went up. He says "here is a piece of property, dont say anything about it, I have got a chance to buy. What is your opinion about it"? I asked what it could be bought for. I dont know as he told me direct, but about. I says "I think it is good property in the heart of the city. The city will be coming towards it all the time. I believe it will be a good investment." Several months afterwards, one Sunday, he says "John, I did as you told me to". I says "what is that," I forgot all about it. "I bought that Birch land."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I wish you would recall the conversation about the will as explicitly as you have this.

speaker: John Morse
testimony: That is all he said about the will, he thought of making some bequests out, you know, for charitable purposes. His farm over there, he was talking about the Old Ladies Home, "I dont know but I would give them this, if they would take it."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that the same talk?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I dont think it was the same time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he talk to you any other time about a will?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: I think that is all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is the first and last time?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: Years ago, out West at my place one time, he said he had a will; several years ago he told me he had destroyed it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long ago did he tell you he had destroyed it?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: 15 years ago.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he tell you anything about the contents of the will?

speaker: John Morse
testimony: He did not.Hosea Knowlton: What is your full name?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Emma L. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What does the L. stand for?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Lenora.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You are older than Miss Lizzie.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your age?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Forty- one.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How old were you, as near as you can recollect, when your father married the second time?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Just a trifle over fourteen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So that you probably remember your Mother?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you lived at home most of the time?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: H ave you ever lived away from home?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I was away at school about a year and a half.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was sometime ago?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go to Europe with your sister?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was it she went?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Two years ago this Summer I think.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Gone how long?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Nineteen weeks.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long had you been away when this tragedy happened?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Two weeks.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Visiting in Fairhaven?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where were you visiting there?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: 19 Green street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Miss Helen Brownell and her mother.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time did you get back?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I came on the train that left New Bedford 3.40.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You came right to the house. You went up to Weir Junction and came down. Who sent for Mr. Jennings?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I think I did. I know I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In relation to Administration?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Not then.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I have no business to ask you what you sent for him for. Did you ever hear your father speak of a will?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you understand that he had a will?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I knew he had had one, I did not know whether it was destroyed or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was it you understood that he had had one?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: O, I dont know, a long while ago.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A number of years?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: More than ten?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Well, I dont know, it seems as if it was longer than that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did you get the information that he had a will then?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: My Uncle, John Morse told me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is not the one that is at the house now?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not hear that it had been destroyed?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is, your Uncle John never told you that?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know anything of the provisions of the will?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was not before your father married the second time, was it?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: That my Uncle told me? No, I dont think it was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it about that time?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: It dont seem as if it was as long ago as that, but I am not sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have never heard your father speak of a will?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you ever talked the matter of the will over with your sister Lizzie? Had you ever talked over the matter of a will of your father with your sister Lizzie?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: We had wondered if there was one, something like that; that was all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When, do you recollect, was the last time the subject was mentioned between you?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I cant tell, I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you away a good deal of the time, or mostly at home?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Mostly at home.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who kept the house, your step mother, that is, who had charge of the management?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She filled the place of housekeeper?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you have, yourself, any particular duty in connection with the house?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Some things I always did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What were they?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I always took charge of the parlor, my sister and I, we always took charge of the guest chamber and our own rooms.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is, you and your sister did that?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not your separate duty, but yours and hers?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I did most of it, sometimes she assisted.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did your mother never have charge of the guest chamber?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I did not know that she ever did. When I was home I dont think she ever did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dont know how it happened that she was having the work of the guest chamber on this morning that she died?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Any other duties that you and Lizzie did about the house, regular duties I mean?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No. I dont think there was anything that we did steadily; just a as we felt, if we wanted to, we did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Lizzie stay at home most of the time?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont know how to answer. Do you mean that she went away out of town visiting?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The question perhaps embraces that and day absences too.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: She was out a good deal days, but she was not away visiting very much.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She did not go out to work?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You mean she was away at other places?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Out visiting friends, and she had a good deal of mission work, and things like that that she did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she usually eat with the family?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Just as it happened. If she was there, sometimes she did, and a good many times she did not get in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she usually have breakfast with the family?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Hardly ever.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did not get up soon enough?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Not always.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: But oftener than she did?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who did the marketing?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Father or Mrs. Borden, I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who usually?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont know, one or the other.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know where the marketing was done?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Didnt have any particular place?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: We always had the groceries from Mr. Wades and John M. Deane's. My sister used to order a great many things from John M. Deane's.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that a meat market?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was the meat usually purchased?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who usually purchased the meat?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Father or Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know anything about those axes that were said to have been found down stairs?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know whether there were two axes down stairs before about this time?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you mean by that that you dont know whether there were or not, or that you know there were not any?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont know whether there were or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: There might have been for all you knew?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had no occasion to use them, of course?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Probably did not have a great deal of business down cellar?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The water closet I believe was down cellar?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Besides that, you did not go down very much?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Not very often. I had nothing to go for.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A short handled hatchet it is said was also found down there, assume that it was, do you know anything about that?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know whether your father kept any such instrument or not?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is, you would not know whether he did or not?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I know the farmer used to come over and cut up wood. I suppose he had something to do it with, but I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Whether any such instrument had been previously kept there, you dont know?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir, I never saw one, but there must have been one.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Assume there were three found?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir, I think I have seen a hatchet down there in the wood room, I am quite sure I have.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You understand, of course, what I mean by a hatchet, a short handled, wide bladed instrument, differs from an ax in being wider bladed.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I guess it is an ax I mean; I dont know which I do mean; if I saw them, I might tell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: An ax is longer handled and smaller bladed. The blade of an ax dont differ much from the shape of that, not quite so long, about that shape. A hatchet is shorter handled, and is more such shape as that blotting paper, the handle going in there, and wider at the bottom.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I cannot tell you which it was, I dont remember.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When do you think you saw, whatever you did see, there?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I havnt any idea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Sometime before that, or recently?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I should say it might have been several months that I had not been in the wood room for anything.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your remembrance of it, so far as you have remembrance, is that it was a hatchet?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I cannot tell which.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You spoke of a hatchet?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: When you describe them, I dont know which it was the most like.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dont know of anything being done with an ax or a hatchet that would cause blood to come on it, do you?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Not unless father killed pigeons with them; I dont know whether he did or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see him kill the pigeons?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Further than that you have no remembrance of anything that would cause blood?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know of anybody that was on unpleasant terms with your father?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Only the rumors that I have heard, since I came home.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: At that time did you know of anybody, before you went away, who was on unpleasant terms with your father?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: The Marshal produces an instrument which is something between an ax and a hatchet. I cant tell, I just know there was something there the farmer used, but I have no idea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I did not hear the answer, if you did make one, to the question I put, whether you knew of anybody that was on bad terms with your father?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: There was one man he did not speak to.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who was that?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Mr. Harrington.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A connection of his first wife?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir, his sister's husband.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did that extend to his siter as well as the husband?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She was on good terms with the family.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Came there to visit?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He did not come?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He did not come to the house?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: But she did?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir. He has been to the door to call my sister or I.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He was not particularly on bad terms with you or your sister?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know of anybody who was not on good terms with your step mother?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir, I dont think I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or of any trouble she had ever had with anybody?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were the relations between you and your step mother cordial?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont know how to answer that. We always spoke.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That might be, and not be at all cordial.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Well, perhaps I should say no then.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were the relations between your sister Lizzie and your mother, what you would call cordial?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I think more than they were with me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Somewhat more than they were with you, but not entirely so, you mean perhaps? I do not want to lead you at all. I judged from your answer you mean that, or dont you mean that? You say somewhat more than your relations were, do you mean they were entirely cordial between your step mother and your sister Lizzie?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Can you tell me the cause of the lack of cordiality between you and your mother, or was it not any specific thing?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Well, we felt that she was not interested in us, and at one time father gave her some property, and we felt that we ought to have some too; and he afterwards gave us some.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That, however, did not heal the breach, whatever breach there was? The giving the property to you did not entirely heal the feeling?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was sometime ago?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir, sometime ago.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long after he gave the property that he did give to your step mother, was it before he gave some property to you and Lizzie?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Well, not very long.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He did it because you and Lizzie claimed that he ought to do it, I suppose? That is, you put in a claim that he should do it?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir we did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How then, did he happen to?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: A friend came to us, and told us that they heard him say that he thought of giving us the homestead on Ferry street, and advised us to ask for it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How often has Mr. Morse been in the habit of coming to the house?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Just as it happened.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is sometimes oftener, and sometimes not so often?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is since he has been here this time?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was not an unusual thing for him to come and spend the night?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: O, no Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know he was coming here this last time?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did he ever write to any members of the family?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: He wrote to me, and I think to father.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A regular correspondence?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: More than once a year, I mean before he came East?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: O, I corresponded with him regularly when he was West.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He was enough of an Uncle then to be a correspondent?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: He is a very dear Uncle of ours, of mine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You corresponded with him regularly?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long has Maggie worked for you? I call her Maggie.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Her name is Bridget.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she go by the name of Maggie?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: My sister and I called her Maggie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long had she worked for you?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I think it is three years this Fall; it may be four.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she use to wait on the table?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Not always.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I mean in the sense of standing around the table as a waiter does?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: We generally waited on the table ourselves. She would come in occasionally and bring anything we needed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She did not serve as a stand up waiter, did not stand behind the party. When you got home, was she at home?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Thursday night, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did she tell you about it?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: She did not tell me anything. I dont remember asking her but one question, two questions.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was that, please?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I asked her if she would stay with us.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: If the other one has no more to do with this matter than that, I dont care for it.

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I asked her if she saw any boy come with a note. I do not remember asking her any other questions.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You never have seen the note that you speak of?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have not been able to find who sent it?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I have not tried.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you looked for the note?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know whether your step mother was in the habit of destroying her letters?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont know, but I think she did, I am not sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she receive many?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Not that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did your father use to preserve his letters, mostly? Did your father preserve his letters usually?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont think he did, unless they were business letters.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After you got home that night, you did not hear Maggie say anything about where she was when the thing happened?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nor have any talk with her at all?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see your sister then when you came home?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did she say about it?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I dont know, there was so much going on.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I dont think I will trouble you with that question any how. Did you use to keep the back door, the screen door, fastened, was that the habit of the household?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Intended to when they were in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How about the front door?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: We locked that with a spring lock.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did the spring lock work?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Once in a while we would find it did not, but very seldom.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The few times it did not work, did it not come from the fact the door was not slammed too enough to catch the spring?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: I suppose that must have been it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had not had it repaired?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It is in the same condition now it had been?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have lived there since the tragedy; you are still living in the house?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It works as well now as it did; there is no difference in it?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: So far as I know, it does.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it the habit to keep the front door locked with the spring lock?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How about the bolt and the big lock?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: We used those only when we went to bed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did they get unlocked in the morning?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: Usually when my sister or I came down stairs, one or the other unlocked them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I understand you are not feeling well?

speaker: Emma Borden
testimony: No Sir, not very well.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So I have omitted a good many questions I should have asked you on that account.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Dr. Seabury W. Bowen?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You are a physician?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You lived next to the Bordens?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long have you lived there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I lived across the street from Mr. Borden twenty years.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You undoubtedly were well acquainted with the family?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And intimately so?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Well, yes, neighbors.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were their family physician?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: They did not need much doctoring?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you attended Mr. Borden recently, previous to his being killed?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you given him any bismuth powders, or anything of that sort?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know whether he had such things around the house?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you ever prescribed them for him?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or for any member of the family?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Not that I remember of, I dont think I have.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So far as you know, was he a man that knew enough about that branch of science to take medicine himself, if he had occasion to?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: That is a question I could not answer; I am sure I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Assuming that bismuth was found in his stomach, you would not have any idea how it got there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had not been called that week to the family?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir, I had not been called. I went over to see them. The day before, Wednesday morning, about eight o'clock, or before eight, Mrs. Borden came to the door and said she was frightened, said that she was afraid she was poisened. I told her to come in. She sat down, and she said the night before about nine o'clock she and her husband commenced to vomit, and vomitted for two or three hours until twelve, I understood.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What morning was this?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Wednesday morning. I asked her what she had eaten for supper, and she told me. She said she had eaten some baker's white bread, and she had heard of baker's cream cakes being poisonous, and was afraid there was something poisonous in the bread that made her vomit. She said she only ate cake and baker's white bread. At that time she had a sort of an eructation of vomiting, slightly. I was afraid she was going to vomit there, I rather got ready for her. I told her to go home, and told her what to take; and she took it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you recollect what it was you prescribed for her?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I told her to take some castor oil, and take it in a little port wine to take the taste off, and probably that would be all she would want. I think immediately after breakfast, I thought they were neighbors, I would just go over. Before that, she said Lizzie came down, she heard them vomiting, I think she was in the next room, and she was up too, and she commenced to vomit at that time, about twelve. I thought if they did not call me I would go over and make a friendly call. I went over after breakfast. I think Bridget let me in, I am very sure it was the front door. I says "Mr. Borden, what is the matter?" He looked at me and wanted to know if anybody had sent for me. I told him no, Mrs. Borden was over, I thought I would just come over and see. He seemed well enough then. He said he felt a little heavy, and did not feel just right, but said he did not think he needed any medicine. I did not urge him at all, of course, and I went home. I did not think much about it. I saw Mr. Borden out two or three hours afterwards. When I went in, I saw Lizzie run up stairs. Mrs. Borden I did not see, because I had seen her before.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see Lizzie that morning?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: She was just going up stairs as I went in the front door; I thought it was her, I am not quite sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was somebody you thought was Lizzie?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir, somebody I supposed was Lizzie, I did not see her face.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And did not talk to her?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see them again to speak to them before they were killed?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No, I did not see any of them to speak to them, I dont think, I am very certain I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you afterwards see Mr. Borden, did you see him Thursday?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont remember of seeing him Thursday. I might possibly. I saw him Wednesday, walking along between the side door and gate. Lizzie I saw walking up the street, and I concluded they were all right, all of them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dont recollect seeing Mr. Borden out on the street Thursday at all?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont recollect it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see Mrs. Borden on Thursday?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No, I did not see her Thursday. I dont remember.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see Lizzie on Thursday before you were called in?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont think I did. I dont remember seeing any of them. I started off probably at nine o'clock.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You go out on your travels at nine o'clock, or thereabouts?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I intend to.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Get back when, or no habit about it?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I intend to be around probably once an hour, if I can.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the first information that you had of the tragedy.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I drove up to my house, and my wife came to the door, and made a motion, and says "they want you quick over to Mr. Borden's." The same time I think Thomas Bowles, a man that works across the street told me, at the same time, I dont know which was first. I thought probably they were worse, so I went right over quick.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you got there who did you find?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I found Miss Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Anybodyelse?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Bridget.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were the first outsider then to get there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: So far as I know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Mrs. Churchill had not then got there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Miss Lizzie speak to you?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Tell exactly what happened now.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I went to the door, and I met them in the hall, I went in the side door, I thought I would get in there quicker, I was so much in a hurry I happened to go that way. I met Miss Lizzie in the hall, and Bridget. I says "Lizzie what is the matter?" I spoke pretty quick. I says "what is the matter Lizzie?" She said she was afraid her father had been stabbed or hurt. I think the word stabbed was used. I says "has there been anybody here"? She said no, not as she knew of, I would not say that she said "no", I take that back; she said not as she knew of, I think. She said she had overheard her father several times talking loud recently, and said she was afraid some of the tenants had had some trouble with him. That is just as near as I can remember it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That conversation took place immediately?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: That took place immediately, the first thing. I says "where is he?" The door was shut, as it usually is, I never saw it open hardly, between the kitchen and the sitting room. I went through the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You said "where is he", what answer did she give?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: In the sitting room. She beckoned along through the dining room. I went through the dining room to the door between the dining room and the sitting room, that was directly at the head of the sofa. As soon as I got at the door, I could see the whole room, and saw him. Of course I was prepared for something awful, as I did not hear him, and there was no sound. He lay there still, unrecognizable, his face was cut in such a manner I never should have known who it was. I stepped in a second, looked around the room to see if there was any disturbance. It struck me like a flash to see if there was anything left in the room. Then I went right back again, and told them that their father was dead. I took hold of his pulse, and found he was dead, satisfied myself. I did not know that he was when I saw him. I took hold of his pulse, and found he was pulseless. Then I went back and informed them that he was dead.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then what? Then what did she say to that, or what did she do?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I cant tell, I dont know. I was stunned myself.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was said about the mother, if anything?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: There was an inquiry made, I dont know whether I made it, somebody said, where is Mrs. Borden, where is Mrs. Borden? Lizzie said, I think, I think she said "she had a note this morning to go and see a sick friend". That is all; such a serious affair as that, I did not stop, and could not do anything, I was satisfied of that, In that time I said I must go and get some of the officers right off, perhaps I said I would get the officers. Lizzie said that Emma was in Fairhaven, and wanted me to telegraph to her. Before I went out she said if you telegraph to her, perhaps she will come on this noon train. I went directly across to my house, and told my wife, and told her about telegraphing. I was satisfied she could not come on the noon train direct, so my boy drove me down to the telegraph office, and I telegraphed to Miss Emma Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had not then heard that the mother had been killed too?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir. Then I went across to Baker's drug store, I motioned the boy to come along. I stopped two or three minutes there and told them of it. When I came out, I got in my carriage again and drove directly to the house again and stopped at Mr. Borden's door and went in. There were no crowds there at that time, that I remember of. I drove up on his side and told the boy to stand there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had the crowd began to collect around there then?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The news had not got around?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir. I dont know as I saw anybody going in at that time. Then I went directly to the same side door, and was met there by Mrs. Churchill and Alice Russell, I think, Mrs. Churchill I am sure. One of them, or somebody, I think Mrs. Churchill, said "they have found Mrs. Borden". I said "where"? She said she was up stairs "you better go up". Then I went up stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Up the front stairs?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, went up the front stairs. As I got at the top of the stairs, as soon as I got up on the second story, I could look right over the deb, and I saw her lying there flat, prone. My thought was, that she had run up there and fainted. I went right around the foot of the bed and satisfied myself in an instant that she was not living. I dont know whether I got hold of her pulse, but I satisfied myself some way, I dont know how, that she was not living. I went right down stairs again and told them Mrs. Borden was dead, killed the same instant, I think I said that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you looked over the bed and saw the form on the floor, where were you, in the hall way?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I think I must have been. I dont think I should probably have looked until I got up to the top of the stairs. I could, if I had been looking, have looked as soon as my head got level, I might then have seen her under the bed. I went directly up fast, and as soon as I looked in the door, I looked over the bed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So where she was lying, you could see it from the head of the stairs?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Not exactly. You would have to come up the head of the stairs and go back a little.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You went down stairs, and what then?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I told them she was dead, then it is pretty confused, the rest of it along there for sometime. I could not say who they were exactly they kept coming and going. I told Bridget to get a sheet, and covered up Mr. Borden, and kept the door shut so the women would not see, and let the men go along.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you came back from going down street, who did you find there besides those you named?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I named Mrs. Churchill and [Miss] Lizzie Borden, and Miss Russell and Bridget. Do you mean when I came back from up stairs?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: No, down town.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Whether Mr. Sawyer was there before I got back from down town, or directly after, I cannot tell myself. Mr. Charles Sawyer was there very near I think the time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was he a neighbor?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No, I dont know where he does live, I have known him sometime.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you came down from up stairs, were more there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, my wife was there then. I told her to go right home.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had anybody else besides your wife come?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I could not swear to that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: If you are not sure Mr. Sawyer had got there when you came in from down town, was he there when you came down from up stairs?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I know he was there almost the first man, I could not swear to that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long before the first police officer came, pretty soon after that?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont know whether Mr. Allen came before Sawyer or not. I dont think he was there before I started down town.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Before you came back from down town?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I could not say.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So far as you remember now, excepting the three that you have named, Sawyer possibly, and Mrs. Churchill, and Alice Russell, were the only ones that had got there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: And Bridget.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: No outsiders had got there but those three, if they had all got there, when you got back from down town?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont remember of seeing anybody. I would not want to say.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It did not take you a great while to go down and back?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I drove right down to the telegraph office quick.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your horse was there all ready?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You notified the police office?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I notified somebody there to get the police, I told them at the house.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you came back?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir, at the house.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The second time you came to the house the crowd had not begun to collect?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont remember of seeing anybody on the sidewalk, there was no carriage there as I drove up under the tree close to the door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You do not remember of seeing Morse when you got back the second time?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nor when you got down from up stairs?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir, not directly. I did not see him for some little time, I dont know when.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had the crowd begun to collect a great deal when he came?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, a good many were there, I cant say who, people that I knew; I knew almost everybody that came in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Bridget have anything to say when you first went into the house, at all?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont know, I cant remember.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was Mr. Borden's body warm when you felt of it?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: There was nothing that attracted my attention. The main thing was, I found he was dead.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which would more likely attract your attention, because coldness would attract my attention.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: He was pulseless; that is what would attact me. If he was cold, it would attract my attention.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You infer inferentially that he was not cold?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont think he was cold. I think if he had been cold, it would attract my attention probably; it might, and might not. I felt of his pulse, and found he was pulseless, and found he was dead. I did not stop for a minute in the room, I dont suppose.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: As he looked to you when you went in, was it pretty plain that he was badly cut up?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: O, yes sir. I did not recognize him, I should not have recognized him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was the blood on his face?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, the blood was on his face, and his eye ball cut directly in two. He lay on his right side, the right side of his face was not seen. I saw the whole of the cut then, and none of the healthy part.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you recollect how Lizzie was dressed that morning?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: It is pretty hard work for me. Probably if I could see a dress something like it, I could guess, but I could not describe. It was sort of a drab, or not much color to it to attract my attention, sort of a morning, calico dress I should judge.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she change her dress?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: She changed her dress sometime in the course of two or three hours. I noticed she had on a wrapper I think afterwards. She did not do that until she went up into her room to stay.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did she go up to her room to stay?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: It is pretty hard saying what time. Very soon after the crowd, I should say. It is pretty hard saying how long. I could not tell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you first came, you all came to the side door?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was she standing, exactly?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I could not say exactly whether she met me at the end of the entry, or where. I could not say. I have the impression now that she was at the side door, and opened the screen door, and the door was half opened, and ready for me; that is my impression.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was Bridget?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: She was very near. The entry is very short, and she was very near. I could not say exactly.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was Miss Lizzie's appearance and manner?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Well, she was excited, and well it is hard to describe it she was somewhat excited; in a hurry for me to come.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In tears?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was Miss Bridget's appearance and manner?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: She was calm, so far as any excitement in her face was concerned, she was uneasy and excited in action, but not in her face. I dont think I am prepared to say much about that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Perhaps you did not take much notice of Bridget.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I did not take much notice of either of them; the main thing was to see what the matter was, and get at it quick.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The conversation with relation to Mr. Borden's having had trouble with some man was all right there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, before I went in to see him. She said Mr. Borden I am afraid is injured, or father, I dont know whether it was Mr. Borden or father; "I am afraid he is stabbed". And just the same as I have said.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was is not very apparent when you went in that he was not only stabbed, but very much cut up; was it not very apparent that he was very badly cut up?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you attended Miss Lizzie since that time?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Since it was done, yes, I have seen her every day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Has she had any physical difficulties, been ill? I ask mostly on account of the newspapers, they say she has had fainting fits.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Nothing more than I should expect, nothing special, no more than I should expect from anybody that is undergoing a severe strain.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Has not fainted?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir, not fainted. By the way, I might put in, it does not connect, but I will go back to the time when I came down the second time. I can describe her condition at that time when I came down stairs the second time, after I found the mother was dead. Miss Russell was at her side in a chair, and was working over her at that time; in a minute or two she got her into the dining room on a sofa or lounge, on the end of that. She was lying down, and she was fanning her, and had wet cloths put on her head, and they were working over her in that way.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was she faint?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Not as I know of at that time. As I found two people murdered there, I supposed the rest would take care of themselves pretty well.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you known of anybody working around the house there recently before that?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir, I did not know of anybody. If they had been, I dont know as I should have known it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know whether Mr. Borden kept axes or hatchets?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: O, no sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not go into his affairs enough for that?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir, never went into the cellar in my life as I remember of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you intimate enough with the family to know what the relations were between Mr. and Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: In what way?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: As husband and wife, were they cordial and intimate and affectionate?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: So far as I ever saw, I never saw anything to the contrary; that is all I can tell, of course.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had no reason to suppose to the contrary?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you enough knowledge of the relations to know what the relations were between Miss Emma and her step mother?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: From all I ever saw or heard directly myself, I dont know anything but what they were. If you want hear say, I can tell all there is in the papers.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did you know of the relations between Miss Lizzie and the step mother?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I think the relations were, I should judge by what I have seen, seeing them together a good deal, I should judge that the relations were harmonious and perfect and natural.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever hear Lizzie say anything about her step mother?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont think I ever did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did she address her, do you remember?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Well, I dont know, I am sure I dont remember. I never was in the house, except on business affairs, and connected with sickness.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Suppose Miss Lizzie and Miss Emma should say that the relations were not pleasant, would you be surprised, from what you have seen of it?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: If they should say so, I dont know anything to the contrary. I dont know anything in favor of it. I spoke from all I ever saw and heard, directly, myself.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Miss Lizzie tell you at any time where she was when this thing happened?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: The first time when inquiry was made where Mrs. Borden was?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: No, did she tell you where she was herself when her father was killed?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, she said the first time, or I wont say when, very early, that she was out of doors, out to the barn, had been out a while.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she say how long she had been there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No, I dont think she did, I dont remember of her saying how long. I dont doubt but I asked the question where she was. She said she was out in the barn.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Didnt she say what she was doing there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: She told me sometime, I dont know whether just then or a short time afterwards, that she was looking after some irons, or tin. The remark she made the first time was she was looking after some irons, not flat irons, I mean irons, it might have been pieces of iron, "irons" was the words used. Says I "where was Bridget"? I don't know but I asked Bridget. Somebody said, one of them said, Bridget had been washing windows, and she went up stairs, and was up stairs. When those questions were asked, whether it was before I went down to telegraph, or after, I dont know. I have the impression it was before.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was this that Lizzie told you she was out around looking for iron?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I have the impression it was very soon after I went over first.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you the first one to see Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir, I was not. I was sent up by Mrs. Churchill. Mrs. Churchill says "we have found Mrs. Borden". I says "where is she"? "She is up stairs, go right up."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you notice then whether the front door was shut or not?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: If it had been open I think I should have known it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not attempt to go in or out the front door?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not notice whether the front door was open or not?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: If it had been open, I think I should have noticed it, I dont think it was open.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Miss Lizzie have her meals sent up? You say she went to her room and stayed there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, they got some toast. I think they went over to my house, across the street and toasted some white bread for her. I dont think she ate it, I dont know about that though; perhaps they got some tea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was Dr. Abbott there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Dr. Abbott was there some time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not that day?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, that day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I got a rumor that some comment was passed between you and him about the size or quantity of the amount of food that was sent up to her.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Between Dr. Abbott and I? Never a word, I would swear to that, not a word. I did not know anything about it. Never anybody, except my wife, has mentioned the fact. I knew Alice, I think, came over and got some tea and white bread toast. Then I think my wife said she did not eat any of it, or something to that effect. I accidentally heard of it, that is all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was Mr. Borden a man of quick temper?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Well, no, I should not hardly think he was a man of quick temper.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: If they are going up stairs, does not that front door, if it is open, does it not shut off the stairs? You cannot go up stairs when the door is open, you have to shut the door to get up, the hall is so narrow, is not that so?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
