testimony: Perhaps so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Dont it open back against the stairs?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Perhaps it does. I should rather think it did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: At any rate you did not have to get rid of the front door to get up stairs?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir. If the door had been open, I should have seen it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your impression is that the door was shut?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes, a very decided impression that the door was shut.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The marshal reminds me to ask you, dont you think on recollection, the first man that got there after you did, was Officer Allen?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I could not say. It might have been.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember of saying to Mr. Allen "go and get the police as fast as you can?"

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I said that to somebody, I was excited.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you been able to fix the time when you first went over to the house?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I have changed my mind on the time. I had no means only guess at first, but I deliberated, and finding out as near as I can, I must have been. I must have stopped my horse in front of my house not very far from ten minutes past eleven. That is about ten minutes earlier than I thought at first. But on asking my boy, he told me where I was at a certain time when the clock struck. By thinking it over sufficiently I think it was earlier than my first impression. My first impression was just an impression.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was your first impression?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Between 20 and 25 minutes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: On further reflection?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: On further reflection, and judging from what my boy told me where I was, and who we met at a certain time, I think it must have been quite a good deal earlier.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: About what time do you now think it was?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Not very far from ten minutes past eleven. It might have been five or ten minutes either way, when I drove up to the house and went in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you got the news and went in?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir. I could not have stayed in there more than five minutes. I went directly to the house, and down to the telegraph office, and must have been back at half past eleven.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You think you did stay in the Borden house five minutes?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont believe I was more than five. I am only guessing.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you think it was more than two?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: It might not have been.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who was the first person you notified of the thing?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: The first I spoke was in Baker's drug store, Samuel Flint was in the store, stood at the corner, I told him Mr. Borden was murdered.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was on your first visit down town?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I went directly from the telegraph office right to the store across the street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You wrote a dispatch here?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I wrote a dispatch, and paid for it, and sent it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you looked at the dispatch to see what time it was?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir, I have not. I suppose the time would be on that, that would show exactly the time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was the first thing you did afterwards?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I drove directly to the office from my house.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you just as lief go with Mr. Seaver over there and get out that dispatch, and see if it marks the time?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After writing the dispatch you told Mr. Flint?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes. I went directly across South Main street. I was well acquainted with Mr. Flint, and I told him about it, and then left him and got into the carriage and drove back again to the Borden house, and went in a second time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not notify any policeman or anybody then?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How soon after you got back the second time was it before the officer got there?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I dont know, I could not say.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was you when the clock struck eleven?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: My boy said that I was on South Main street, and we met a fire department team near the head of Union; we heard the clock strike.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You mean your own son?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: My driver.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dont know anything about that message for Mrs. Borden to go out nursing somewhere that day?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is your boy around here now?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Now you figure from that it would take you something like ten minutes to get down.

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: No, it would not take over five.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How do you get at the ten minutes?

speaker: Seabury Bowen
testimony: I leave a lee way of ten minutes, I did not mark it definitely.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your full name?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Adelaide Buffington Churchill.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You live in the next house to Mr. A. J. Borden?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Up or down stairs?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Down stairs, or all over the house, there is only one family.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long have you lived there?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: With the exception of we moved away from the house a while the day the Granite Mill burned in October, and we were away until May, I think, we have lived there the rest of the time, forty-two years.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So you were well acquainted with the Bordens?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Very well acquainted.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do your windows look out on their yard?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: On the south side of the house they do.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is that the side where your living part is?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: The kitchen was on the south side and two lodging rooms. The sitting room windows face the west, one long window in the sitting room faces south.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you at home that Thursday morning?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I was down street. I went from the house near eleven o'clock to do my marketing.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know where the Bordens marketed?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont. I think to Whitehead's, I am not sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who usually did the marketing for the Bordens, Mr. or Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Mrs. Borden went a good many times out. I dont know that, I dont know their private affairs at all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Up to eleven o'clock had you been at home?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I was at home until about eleven.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you seen any of the household, or anybody else in the yard?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I saw Mr. Borden I should think about nine o'clock, the hour he usually goes down street. I was in my kitchen doing kitchen work. I happened to see him out in the yard.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You saw him going out?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes, he was going as if he had been out in the yard, out by the barn, coming out around the back steps.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He went out on the street?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont know. When I looked at him he was standing there by the steps as if he was headed for the street, to the west.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see him go to the street?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, I was tending my work, I did not look after he went by.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see any other member of the household?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir. I saw the girl later washing the windows.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much later was it she was out washing windows?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: It might have been ten o'clock. I cant tell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Washing windows on the outside?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long should you say she was out there, that you saw her washing windows?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I cant tell. I stepped into my bed room for something, I saw her throwing water up on to the parlor window.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She was washing the parlor window then?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember how long before that time she had been out washing the windows, whether that same week, or the week before?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont think she washed windows but once a week, and Thursday was generally the day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was the habit to wash the windows once a week?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: She generally did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see her wash any other window beside the parlor window?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see any other member of the household beside Mr. Borden and the girl?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see anything of Mr. Morse?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did not see him go away?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nor you did not see Miss Lizzie out in the yard?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or any other person not a member of the household, before the tragedy?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, I did not see anybody pass out there that morning.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the first thing to which your attention was attracted? I was coming down Second street, I had been to Hudner's market. I got about half way between Mrs. Burts house and mine, and I saw Bridget going from Dr. Bowen's door over to the Borden House, running, and she looked as if she was scared.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was the first?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How far were you then from the house?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Half way between Mrs. Burts house and mine. I went in my back gate.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you twice the length of this room?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: It was a good deal further than that corner.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Twice as far perhaps?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I cant get at distances.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time, as near as you can judge from everything, was that?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont know, I think it was about eleven o'clock when I started out of the house, somewhere in that vicinity. I knew I had got to get down street and get back time enough to get potatoes for dinner, so to have them at twelve o'clock. I only gave a short order, did not stay long there, and came right back.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the first thing you did?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I went into the house, stepped through to the kitchen, laid my parcels on a bench which runs right across one of the south windows that looks into Mr. Borden's back yard. At the screen door, standing by the screen door I saw Lizzie as if she was in great distress.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did she show that?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Perhaps she rubbed her head. I knew something was wrong, of course, by the appearance. I opened one of the south windows, one had a screen in and the other did not, I says "what is the matter Lizzie?" She said "O, Mrs. Churchill, do come over, somebody has killed Father." I went right through the house and went out the front door and went over. When I got there she sat on the second stair which is right at the right of the screen door as you come in, the back stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Crying?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir. I put my hand on her arm, this way, and said "Lizzie, where is your father"? She says "in the sitting room". I said "where was you when it happened?" She said "I went to the barn to get a piece of iron." I said "where is your mother"? She says "I dont know, she had a note to go and see some one that was sick this morning, but I dont know but they have killed her too." She said "father must have had an enemy, for we have all been sick, and we think the milk has been poisoned." The she said "Dr. Bowen is not at home, but I must have a Doctor". I says "shall I go and try to find someone to go and get a doctor"?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was that?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: She said "Dr. Bowen is not at home, and I must have a Doctor." I says "shall I go, Lizzie, and try to find someone to go and get one"? She said yes. I went out and ran across the street, because I knew the young man that worked for us had gone in a yard where they sell horses, Mr. Hall's yard, with a carriage he had been washing in our yard. He was going out of the yard that day when I went in with my bundles. I went across and called for Thomas Bowles. I says "somebody has killed Mr. Borden; go and get a Doctor." I dont know where he went. I went back. Soon after I got back she says "I shall have to go to the Cemetary myself". "O, No," I says, "Lizzie, the undertaker will attend to all such things as that for you; they generally do." Very soon Dr. Bowen came in, and I said to him "he is in the sitting room." Dr. Bowen went directly to the door that opens from the kitchen into the dining room, at the right of the door as you come into the back entry, just beyond it, and Lizzie and Bridget and I, Bridget had come back, she had been for Alice Russell, a friend of Lizzie's, and had got back, and Lizzie, Bridget and I went as far as the dining room. Lizzie sat down on the lounge, Bridget and I stood there. Dr. Bowen went into the sitting room, came out and shook his head and says "that is awful". He said to me, he was very much confused, "Addie", he says, "wont you go in and look at Mr. Borden"? I says "O, no Doctor, I dont want to see him. I looked at him out in the yard this morning, he looked nice to me, I dont want to see him." He says "perhaps it is best you should not", or "it is just as well." Lizzie says "Doctor, will you send a telegram to Emma, my sister, for me"? Ie says "I will do anything for you". She wished him not to tell the worst, if he could help it, for the old lady where Emma was visiting was feeble, she had better not have the shock. He went out. I think a policeman by the name of Allen came in back of him, and Charles Sawyer, a man that lived below us on Second street, Miss Russell had got there in the mean time, and asked Mr. Sawyer if he would please tend the door so that no one would come in that had not ought to come in, so we stayed in the kitchen. I found Alice Russell sat down on a rocking chair, with Lizzie, and Lizzie had her head on Alice Russell's shoulder. Mrs. Dr. Bowen came in in a few moments, and came and stood beside me. Miss Lizzie did not seem to know Mrs. Dr. Bowen, she asked who that woman is. Miss Russell said "that is Phebe Bowen." Bridget was in the kitchen at the time. Lizzie says "I wish someone would go and try to find Mrs. Borden. Dr. Bowen said he would like a sheet to cover up Mr. Borden. Bridget was to go and get it. Lizzie told her to go up in a small room, adjoining her mother's room, I think she called it a dressing room. I went with Bridget, because she did not want to go alone. I stood in the door way of the little room while she went to the bureau and took out two sheets. She says to me "is two enough"? I says "I should think a plenty". We came down stairs, and Dr. Bowen took one, and the other sheet was laid on the dining room table. I am not sure but she handed me both of the sheets, and I laid one on the dining room table. Dr. Bowen went in, I presume he covered up Mr. Borden next. Then Lizzie said she wished someone would go and try to find Mrs. Borden up stairs, so I went with Bridget. I think Bridget went ahead of me. I got half way up the front stairs, I got just far enough so my head was level with the front entry floor, I turned my head to the left, and in turning my head to the left, I could see straight across the spare bed room floor, and at the north side of the bed I saw something that looked like a prostrate form of something. I could distinguish nothing, the room was not light, it was a little darker, darker than down stairs. It looked more than any mat would be on the floor. I turned around and went back. I dont know whether I said out loud "that must be her". I think Bridget went up stairs, how far she went, I dont know, because I was so shocked. I went down stairs, went into the dining room, and Alice Russell says, "is there another"? "Yes, she is up there". When Dr. Bowen came in again I says "Dr. Bowen, you must go up stairs in the spare bed room". And he went.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That part happened while he was out?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont think he was in there, I cant tell certainly. I told him when he came in that he must go up stairs. After that, there was a great deal of confusion. I did not stay very much longer after that. I went into the dining room, told Lizzie I must go home. If there was anything I could do for her, I was willing to do it. She said perhaps there would be something later that I could do.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Before Dr. Bowen came, you came? You came before he did?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes, we stood in that entry. I went again to find a doctor before he got there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You got back the second time before Dr. Bowen came?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: While you were gone, you went to where Thomas Bowles was?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes, to L. L. Hall's boarding stable on the opposite side of the street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was after you got back the second time Dr. Bowen came?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir, then Miss Russell soon came.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: While you were gone down to Bowles', there was nobody there, so far as you know, but Lizzie and the servant?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I left her alone when I went, the servant had not got back from the other errand.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The first one that came after you got back the second time, was Dr. Bowen.

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nobody went into the sitting room to see the body until Dr. Bowen went in?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, not that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you looked and saw Lizzie in great distress, the first time, was she standing inside the screen door?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: That was before I went over, and I was in my own house, and she stood by the screen door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Outside the door or inside?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, inside, and the door was closed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Then you ran out the front door, around through the yard, and right over to where she was?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir, from my house.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did you get through the screen door, did she unhook it?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I think I opened it myself. She sat on the stairs as I went in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was not locked?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, the girl had run again, after she came from Dr. Bowens.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see Mr. Morse before you went home?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir, he came before I went home.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: About what time in the order of events did he come?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Both Mr. and Mrs. Borden had been found when he came. I think I was the first one that let him in. I says "Mr. Morse, something terrible has happened, somebody has killed both Mr. and Mrs. Borden." He says "what", and hollered "Lizzie", as loud as he could holler, and rushed into the dining room. Alice Russell heard him, and I think let him in, and he went into the sittig room and the door was closed between the sitting room and the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was after both were discovered?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it pretty soon after both were discovered?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it before or after Mr. Allen got there?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Mrs. Borden was not discovered until after Mr. Sawyer got there, because I told Mr. Sawyer what we thought we had found up stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was there much of a crowd there when Mr. Morse got home?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Quite a number out in the yard, they had not got into the house, a great many of them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did Mr. Morse come from?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: The back part of the yard, or from the street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you first see him?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: He stepped up on to the steps. I think he came from the east, the steps that come from the yard, but I am not sure, I cant tell you surely.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: As your memory is, there were some people in the yard at the time?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I think the crowd had come; it is my impression they had. I dont know how much of a crowd there was until I went home.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Can you give any impression you think would be of value as to how long a time elapsed after the finding of the first body before the finding of the second?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, I have no idea of that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You knew nothing about the internal affairs of the household?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir. They were always the pleasantest to me whenever I was there, whenever I called there, I never passed pleasanter calls than in that house, by every member.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not know about what tools they kept down cellar?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Nothing.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know anything about the relations between the girls and their father and mother?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever notice anything out of the way about them?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever notice that they were cordial?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I never thought they were as cordial as they might be, but I dont know nothing about it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was it you observed that led you to think they were not?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Because I never saw them together.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Anythingelse?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, I think nothing I can say that I know anything about. I know nothing. They always spoke in the pleasantest terms of each other when I saw them. I know nothing further.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So far as you can observe, they went their way, and she went her way?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes, that is all I know about it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you happen to see Bridget washing the windows inside the house that morning?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Lizzie said she was in the barn getting a piece of iron?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she explain to you then what she was going to do with the iron?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, I never asked her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And she never has explained it?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you now told all you know of all that happened in your presence with relation to the affair?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And all that was said in your presence?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I cannot tell you when she said it, I am very particular what I tell, she said once she wished somebody would try to find Mrs. Borden because she thought she heard her come in. Whether she sat on the stairs when she said it, I cant tell you. She thought she heard her come in. I cant tell when she told it to me, whether before or after I went after the Doctor; she said it to me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You are sure you did hear it?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: O. yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that the occasion of your going to look for Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir. After that she said again she wished we would go up stairs and see if we could find Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When she said she thought she had heard her come in, was said before she said she wished you would go up stairs?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes, before that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you now told all that was said in your presence, that you have heard?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see around there any indications of blood, such as bloody cloths, or cloths with blood on them?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I only went in the kitchen and entry until Dr. Bowen came in, and I saw nothing.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: No indications, bloody cloths or anything?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir, and she looked clean, perfectly clean.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What dress did she have on, Lizzie?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: She had on a blue and white calico. Blue and white with a deeper navy blue diamond on it; of a deeper shade of blue, this diamond was, as near as I can tell it; I am not observing of clothes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is the pattern of the cloth?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir, the diamond was printed on it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she have on an apron?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont think she did, but I cant tell you certain.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Are you sure it was calico?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No, it might be gingham; it is cotton goods I think.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It could not have been india silk, or anything of that pattern?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No, I dont think it was; it was calico or gingham I think. I do not think she would wear an india silk in the morning, unless she was going out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you think you would know the dress if you saw it again?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I think I should, I never saw it near to, though, until that day.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have described it as fully as you can?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it all one piece, like the dress you have on now?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: It was a looser waist, more like a blouse waist.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: All one kind of cloth, as you are now?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not as she (reporter) is, but all one kind?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see any ironing board in the dining room?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see whether it was there or not?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont think it was, but I am not very observing.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not carry the ironing board out?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see any signs of ironing anywhere?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I did not, to my knowledge.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you notice the flats on the stove?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You mean by that, you did not notice anything about it, not that they were not there?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I did not notice anything about it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who took charge of things, of matters there after this tragedy? Did anybody come in there that took charge of things?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you mean nobody did, or you dont know?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I dont know of anybody. Miss Russell looked out for Lizzie, and took her in the dining room soon after she came in, and the dining room door was shut. If anybody wished to see her, they had to go in there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You are pretty sure, Mrs. Churchill, that, as you remember it, the waist was the same kind of stuff as the skirt?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I am. I told you that I am not observing as a great many.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You took it to be the same?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not different kind of cloth of a similar color?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You say the waist was something of the blouse order?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: Loose.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Belted in?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I cannot tell you how it was made. I know it hung loose here, because I know Miss Russell thought she would open it to give Lizzie a better chance to breathe. Lizzie says "my clothes are loose."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you not notice then the dress was the same kind as the skirt?

speaker: Adelaide Churchill
testimony: I saw the waist had spots on it of the dark blue. I had seen it before at a distance when she came to the door, from my window. It was navy blue, with a darker shade blue and white, a diamond printed on the cloth.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your name?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Hiram C. Harrington.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You live in Fall River?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: I do, sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you related or connected with Andrew J. Borden?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: I am not connected with him any more than I married his sister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is your wife living?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: She is.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What part of the city do you live in?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: No. 12 Turner street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is that in that neighborhood?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What part of the town is that?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Up near the depot.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you on familiar terms with all the household?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you with any of them?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which of them were you not?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: All except Mr. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Mr. Borden himself?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Mr. Borden himself.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You and he did not speak? You and he were unfriendly? You and he were not on good terms?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: We never had no words, or anything of that kind. Some years ago I thought he was hard, and I cut his acquaintance; that is, he came to my house, and I would leave the room; and he very soon saw I cut his acquaintance; and he did mine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: But you continued on good terms with the rest of the family?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Yes, friendly to them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That takes in the wife and the daughters?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go to the house any?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: No, I did not go to the house, any more than sometimes on business, that is, sometimes my wife wanted to send a letter or to invite the girls, or something of that kind, I would go to the house; sometimes I met him at the door, and have spoken.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know what the relations were between the daughters and the mother?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: I did not go into the house; all I can tell is hear say, that is from them. The step mother never mentioned it in my presence.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Lizzie?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Lizzie has, yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What has Lizzie said about it?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: I dont know as I could put anything together now to tell you, any more than to tell you there was some difficulty some way. She thought she equivocated. I dont know as I could put enough of it together now, I can just give you an idea. I cant remember words that were passed at the time, any more than just this much, that she thought she equivocated.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: About what, did she say?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: In regard to something about Bertie, that is, Mrs. Whitehead, a half sister of Mrs. Borden. I think it was something about helping her, or that her father had bought the property. The general construction I have got of what she said, and from what little I learned, was that he had bought the property and gave it to his wife; and of course that meant giving it to her half sister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Lizzie speak about it to you more than once?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Sometimes it has been mentioned in a joking way, about the difficulties. I dont know as I could put enough together to say really what was passed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long ago was the last time she said anything about it?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: I think last Winter sometime. I have not seen her at the house for, I might say all Summer, and I have inquired of my wife how it was that Lizzie had not been down. Emma has always come. And the reply I would get from her was that Lizzie was into everything, that is, the works in the church, and her time was occupied; that is what I would get from her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When she spoke about it last Winter, what did she say about it?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: I dont know as I could tell any more than to speak kind of sneeringly of Mrs. Borden. She always called her Mrs. Borden or Mrs. B. I dont know as I could remember anything to put together to make any sense.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she speak in an unfriendly way of her?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Unfriendly, yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You never heard Mrs. Borden say anything about it at all?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Never mention it. I have heard my wife say that Abby never mentioned it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: But it was understood there was trouble in the family?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Oh, yes there has been I guess. For several years, I guess, of his early marriage with her, everything was very, very pleasant, uncommonly so for a step mother.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: This trouble is of recent years?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Quite a number of years, I should think. They were rather reticent about telling these affairs, although sometimes it would crop out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You don't know of anybody that was hostile to Mr. Borden?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Not a person.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Nor Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Nor Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that the step sister that married Whitehead?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: A half sister of Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is the one that married Whitehead that you have been telling about?

speaker: Hiram Harrington
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your name?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Charles S. Sawyer.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where do you live?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: At 78 Second Street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How far is that from the Borden House?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: The third house.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The third house south?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: The third house north.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: This way?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: On the same side of the street?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you acquainted with Mr. Borden?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes Sir I knew him, not intimately; I had had some business with him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you visit the family?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No Sir, I was not in the habit. I have been there in the house. I used to go there once in a while to make some little purchases in vinegar and stuff.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were not a calling acquaintance of the family?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the first you saw that attracted your attention with reference to this matter?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I was in the machine shop opposite where I live, and a gentleman came in there, a drummer from Providence, and he reported that a man named Borden just above here had been stabbed. I started out to make some inquiries, and as I stood talking with three gentlemen at the foot of the stairs, this was up stairs, I saw Miss Alice Russell going up on the other side of the street. Knowing that she had been a near neighbor, and quite intimate with the girls, of course I went over to inquire if she had heard. She told me yes she had heard, and was going right up there; so I followed along to see if I could get any particulars about it. I went as far as the gate. She did not seem to be very talkative, but considerably excited.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Running?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No Sir she was not running, but walking quite fast. I turned to leave the gate, and had got down some little distance from the gate, Officer Allen came along, and I walked along with him a little ways to see if he had heard anything. When he got to the gate he says "you better come right in. I want you to tend the door;" so I followed him in. When we got to the door he says "now you stand right here at the door, and allow no one to go in or out. I want to make some investigation here." I dont know as that was the exact words, but that was the purport of it. So he went in, and went through the house, I supposed, and I stood at the gate all that time. During that time Miss Borden was in the kitchen with Miss Russell, Mrs. Churchill and Dr. Bowen and the servant girl; that was all that were there at that time, except Officer Allen who had gone in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you got in there, who did you find?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I found the servant girl, Lizzie Borden, Miss Alice Russell, the lady I had been up with before, and Mrs. Churchill. Dr. Bowen I think was there, and I could not say whether he had been there before, and just gone out or not, I could not say about that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Allen went in with you?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes, I went in when Officer Allen did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was Miss Borden's appearance?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Miss Borden sat in a chair in the kitchen; she seemed to be considerable excited I thought, as though she was apparently grief stricken, or something, although I might not be a judge in that respect. She seemed to be considerably excited and very uneasy, and the ladies seemed to be ministering to her, that is bathing her face.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Crying?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I cant say that she was crying. I could not tell exactly. I did not go way into the kitchen at all [until] after that some little time. I stood near that door. You know there is quite an entryway. (Judge Blaisdell) Which door?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: At the door facing north, the side door with the screen and hook.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You could see into the kitchen?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I could look into the kitchen. I could not see the whole of it, but I could see the biggest part of it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go into the house?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I walked into the door. I kept the door locked on the inside. I walked in at the door; the cellar door, which was right close there, I bolted it. There was a bolt on the side in the entry. I did not know but from the actions around there, there might possibly be somebody in there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the appearance of the servant girl?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: The servant girl appeared to be, well, somewhat frightened. I thought she acted as though she was considerably excited, although she talked intelligently, anything that was said to her, and she seemed very willing to give any information that she knew anything about.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear the servant girl say anything?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes, I heard her montion something about that note that had been sent.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was that she said?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I heard her say something about Mrs. Borden saying that she had received a note, but I cant seem to recollect just how she put it, whether she said Mrs. Borden told her, or somebody else told her. I also heard Mrs. Churchill mention that fact.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did she say?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I think she said that Mrs. Borden had received a note calling her out. At that time I was not actually sure that Mr. Borden was killed I understood he had been stabbed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not go in to see him?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No Sir, not then. Finally I went in. I says "is he dead"? "Yes", they says, "he is dead."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who said that?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Mrs. Churchill says "O, yes he is dead." Dr. Bowen stood there, and asked me if I would like to see him. I dont know as he opened the door, he turned and pointed in, and I saw a sheet thrown over a body, some little signs of blood on it. Dr. Bowen asked me if I would like to see it. I did not particularly want to see it, but at the same time I went in and I looked at it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear Miss Lizzie say anything?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No, I did not hear Miss Lizzie say but very little, I dont know that I heard her say anything that I could really repeat. She did speak to some of the ladies there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you there when Mrs. Borden was found?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes Sir I was. She was found sometime after I had been there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know how she happened to be found?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Somebody had said, although I was out in the entry at the time, I understood it there was some inquiry made where Mrs. Borden was. Who made that inquiry, I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What reply was made to the inquiry?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I did not hear that. After that inquiry had been made, I stood in the kitchen, just inside of the door, and Mrs. Churchill came up to me, apparently had come from some other part of the house, she says "Mrs. Borden is dying". She seemed to be quite excited. She says "Mrs. Borden is dying, I think from the shock". Where she got her information, I dont know, whether she went up to see or not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you there when Morse returned?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I was there when Morse returned, I suppose the time he returned; whether he had been there before, I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you first see him?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: He came towards me from the gate. I stood on the steps at that time, standing outside of the door and holding the door outside. He came along to the steps, and he says "for Gods sake what has happened here"? I looked at him, I had not seen him, he was a stranger to me, I told him Mr. and Mrs. Borden have been murdered, been killed, something to that effect. "My God", he says, "and I left Mr. Borden right at this door, and he told me to come back to dinner."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long was that after you got there, as far as you can estimate time?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I should judge somewhere in the neighborhood of very near twelve o'clock, or a little after; of either way, I should say a little after.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you see him first?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was anybody with you at the time?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I am not sure about that, I dont think that there was, unless the servant girl might have been inside the door, or somebody inside the door at that time. The servant girl would go out and in there through that entry sometimes, when I was outside.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was there much of a crowd there at that time?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: At that time there was quite a little crowd there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In the house, or out in the street?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: They had been driven out of the yard by an officer there in attendance.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The officers were there then?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes, when he came.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were the people in the street?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Many?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Well, yes sir I should say there was. My view was limited, not more than the width of the yard, but the fences appeared to be pretty well filled up; and previous to that, there had been quite a crowd in the yard.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you notice whether Mr. Morse was eating a pear or not, or eating anything?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No Sir he was not when he spoke to me, at that time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did he do when you told him?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: "My God", he says, "what kind of a God have we got that will permit a deed like this to be done?" Something like that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did he do then?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: He stood there a few minutes, and finally went inside of the door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Whether he had been in before or not, you dont know?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No Sir, he had not been in at that door before; that was the first time I had seen him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had been near the door all the time?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Yes Sir, from the time I went there with Officer Allen, I should judge that might have been a little after eleven.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you any way of fixing the time when the thing was told you by this Providence man?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: Not exactly. While I was up in that machine shop, this gentleman came in and reported it, and he told a man there afterwards that he came from Providence that morning, and had landed at the Mellen House. I judged that he came from the Mellen House direct to his store, I judged from that he came on the half past ten o'clock train, although it seemed to me a little earlier than [that]. I thought I was there about eleven or a little before. I might be mistaken, I did not note, or look at the time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who was the man?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: He sells machinist supplies for a firm in Providence. I suppose Mr. Rich could tell that. I have seen him once or twice there, but I dont know who the man was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is there any other fact you have not told us that you saw or heard, connected with the affair?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I went up to the room where Mrs. Borden was found, and saw her laying there, and saw the blood on the bed, or at least on the shams, apparently a smooch it struck me, on the counterpain or coverlet.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see any more bloody cloths around there?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No, except what was around Mrs. Borden. I did not stop there long, I thought I had got to stay to the door. I stayed around there until six o'clock. While I was there the story was going around with regard to a Portugee or some foreigner that was working on the farm. Miss Alice Russell came to me and said that Miss Lizzie Borden was very anxious that that story should be suppressed, because she said he was a very nice man, and a very old and tired servant in the family, and she was deeply concerned about it. Miss Russell made this talk with me. Lizzie did not say anything about it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was Miss Lizzie present at the time Miss Russell told you that?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: No, she had been sent up to her room at this time. When they came down and reported that her mother had been killed, she apparently went off into some kind of a swoon or hysterical fit, I dont know exactly what, and Dr. Bowen said she better be carried up to her room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which room was she taken to?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I dont know, I did not go up.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it up the front or back way?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I could not say that, either.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who went up with her?

speaker: Charles Sawyer
testimony: I think Miss Russell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your name?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Agusta D. Tripp?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where do you live?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Westport.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you related in any way to Mr. or Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or connected by marriage?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know them?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes sir, I know them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever visit the family?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you first begin to visit there?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: When I was a school girl, I think in the year 1875.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you been visiting them since, more or less?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Very little since.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was the last time you were there, do you think?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: To make a visit?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I went there the 13th of July of last year.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not this year?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir. I made a call there this year, but not a visit.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long did you stay when you were there the 13th of July?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I went Monday and came back Saturday.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were there nearly a week?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was Miss Lizzie at home?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And Miss Emma?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And the old people?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Mrs. Borden I did not see.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was she?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: In Swansea I think they said.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: During the whole time?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Mr. Morse was not there?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The same domestic, Bridget Sullivan, was she there?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: They always called her Maggie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The same girl that worked for them since?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I have not seen her, I suppose so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I did not know but you had seen her since the affair happened?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you seen either Miss Emma or Miss Lizzie since then, very much?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Since that visit, no sir, I have only seen them a very short time, I think less than an hour, that was this Spring I made a call.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was it?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I made a call on them at the house, just made a call.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who did you see then?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Miss Emma and Miss Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Nor Sir I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you acquainted with Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: A very little from meeting her with Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your friends were more particularly the daughters?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you a school mate of either of the daughters?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: With Miss Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So that your most intimate friend in the family was Miss Lizzie?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever hear either Miss Emma or Miss Lizzie say anything about their step mother?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I have heard them speak of her of course.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And about their feeling towards her?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I think I have heard them say but very little.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which one have you heard talk about it?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I think Miss Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was it you heard her talk about it?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I could not tell you when.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it the time of your visit there?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Very little said about her; Mrs. Borden was away, and there was very little said about her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What do you remember of hearing Miss Lizzie say about it?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: About Mrs. Borden then?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Yes, or at any time.

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Well, I dont know what to tell you, for I have seen them so little that they did not go to tell me all their...

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I want to know what they did tell you, that is all.

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: It would be rather hard for me to remember what they tell me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Remember what you can. Do you remember what you told the officer about it, Mr. Medley, did you have a talk with him?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir, I have had a talk with him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember what you told him?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I dont remember that he asked me such a question as that, I answered his questions.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did he ask you?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: He asked me several questions, and I answered them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you any opportunity of observing what the relations were between the daughters and the mother, or between Miss Lizzie and the mother?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Whenever I saw them together, they had very little to say to each other, seemed to have very little to say to each other. Everything went along quietly. They did not seem to make very much conversation with each other.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did they eat at the same table?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: O, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever hear Mrs. Borden say anything about either Emma or Lizzie?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She made no talk?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir. I have seen very little of the family since I have been married, since I have been away from the city.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What can you tell us about the relations between Lizzie and her mother, so far as you observed it, and heard it from Lizzie?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: All I can tell you is that I dont think that they were agreeable to each other.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What made you think so?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I have seen them together very little. What should make me think so, would be if I were there, why, they did not sit down, perhaps, and talk with each other as a mother and daughter might. They were very quiet.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is, they were together so little that you observed the fact?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No, I dont think I should, they were around in the same room together, the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: They associated together so little you noticed the fact they did not associate together?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I noticed it; not that they kept away from each other, not that at all, but that they did not enter into conversation, perhaps, with each other, perhaps.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that so with Lizzie as well as Emma, or with one daughter more than the other?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I think Lizzie talked with her mother more than Emma.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Emma had less to say to her?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What else did you notice that led you to think that Lizzie and the mother did not get along well together, or were not agreeable to each other, as you expressed it?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I dont know of anything, I cant recollect anything.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What you noticed was their manner towards each other?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is all, not from any words?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Their manner to each other was not that of those persons that are agreeable to each other, or it did not seem to be.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was it that you have seen them together?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I could not tell you surely; it is as much as five years since I have seen Mrs. Borden at all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So all this was based on what was quite a while ago?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: O, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The officer reports that you told him that Lizzie told you at some time, that she thought her step mother was deceitful, one thing to her face and another behind her back.

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Did he say I said Lizzie told me so?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I did not think I told him so. It seemed to me so; it seemed to me that she did not like one way appearing to her face, you know deceitful, she could not bear deceitfulness, and she could not bear one thing to her face, and find out another thing to her back; she could not bear deceitfulness.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that what Lizzie told you?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I could not say she told me that, that was the idea I got from what, well, I dont know as I could say from being there, or from being with Lizzie perhaps, for I have been there very little.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You also told the officer that Lizzie told you that her step mother claimed not to have any influence with the father, but Lizzie thought she did have an influence with him.

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Yes, I think Lizzie thought she did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Lizzie tell you that her step mother claimed not to have any influence with him?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I dont remember any such talk.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: With relation to giving some property to the step mother?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Lizzie, from what I have heard her say, but I could not tell you the words, Lizzie said, but I gathered from what I heard her say, it was a long time before I heard her say it, that she thought her mother must have had an influence over her father, or he would not have made a present to her half sister. It was a long time ago, not expecting this to come up, I could not swear to one word Lizzie said.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: This was all prior to the last visit, nothing was said about this at the last visit?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Lizzie say to you she did not know that either Emma or she would get anything in the event of her father's death?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I did not hear her say so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who told you she said so?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I think my invalid sister told me so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is her name?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: Miss Carrie M. Poole, she is very feeble, she lives on Madison street New Bedford, she is very feeble indeed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You never heard Lizzie say that?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir, I never heard Lizzie say that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The officer says you said explicitly, Mrs. Tripp, that Lizzie told you that she thought her step mother was deceitful, one thing to her face, and another thing behind her back, not in so many words, but that was the substance of what she said.

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I dont remember of her saying that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember of telling that to the officer?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: I remember very well talking to him that I thought Lizzie thought her mother was deceitful, one thing to her face, and another to her back. I could not say Lizzie told me that, I cant say so. I was taken very much by surprise at seeing Officer Medley come in, and I tried to tell; but those things were years back, and thinking they never would come up, I cant recollect word for word things that occurred years ago. I cant say that Lizzie told me she thought so; but it would be from little things I might have heard her say that would cause me to think she could not bear deceitfulness, being such an honorable person as she was, square person.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she appear to be fond of her step mother in her talk with you?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No, I dont think she was fond of her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she appear to be unfriendly towards her?

speaker: Augusta Tripp
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your name?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Alice M. Russell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where do you live?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: 33 Borden street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How far is that from the Borden house?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: It is the next square.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Between second and main streets?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir, Borden street is the next street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were well acquainted with the Borden family, all of them?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was it at your house Miss Lizzie was visiting the night before?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Spent the evening there?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did she go home?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think about nine o'clock; it may have been five minutes before or after.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that quite a frequent thing, for her to visit you?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: She has done so more this Summer, because she has not had quite so much outside work, but we have always visited, been friends.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you visited there a great deal?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, sometimes perhaps I would go in quite often, and then again quite a spell I would not go.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you went in, did you see the whole family, or Miss Lizzie or Emma?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I saw the girls mostly.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your acquaintance was mostly with the daughters?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not so much with the old people?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you usually see them?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Up stairs, what they used for a sitting room usually.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which room was that?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: What they call the guest chamber.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was used as a sitting room?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Generally, for them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who used that as a sitting room?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The two girls.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that where they usually sat when they were at home?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is, so far as you know?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: So far as I know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you recollect the last time you visited them?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The last time I visited them, or visited there?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Either one.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I went in there, I am sure, once after Emma had gone to Fairhaven.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who were you more particularly intimate with, Emma or Lizzie, or both?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont think there was very much difference.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the first that you saw or observed, or heard, of this tragedy?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think It was about quarter past eleven when I saw Bridget coming up the steps, and my work is so I can see any one coming up the steps, where I was at work. I knew there was trouble because Lizzie told me Mr. and Mrs. Borden were sick the night before, very sick, so the first impression I got was that somebody was sick there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She told you Tuesday night they were sick?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir. I stepped to the door, and I says "what is it Bridget, are they worse", or Maggie. She says, "yes, I dont know but what Mr. Borden is dead." I dont know whether she said "come over". I dont remember what she said. I said "I will come right over as soon as I change my dress; which I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She did not tell you how he had been killed, or anything of that sort?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: She did not, no.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She said what?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: She says "he is worse, I dont know but what Mr. Borden is dead."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not take much time to change your dress?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You went over as quick as you could?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you got there, who did you find there?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I have a very confused idea. I met Lizzie, at the same time there was somebody else there, who I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Mrs. Churchill?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you meet Miss Lizzie?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think about the threshold of the kitchen door, where the screen door is. No, I think the kitchen door.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: From the hall into the kitchen?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You found the screen door unfastened, and went right in?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did Maggie go back with you?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, she went ahead of me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was said and done when you got there, as well as you remember it?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I have a very confused idea of it. I have tried my best to have it clear. I met Lizzie, and I said "sit right down here Lizzie in the kitchen"; and she sat down. I dont seem to remember what she said or done, except she says "will somebody find Mrs. Borden". She seemed to be very much overcome.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she tell you anything about where to look for her?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir. Then I remember of Maggie and Mrs. Churchill starting, and Maggie says "O, I cant go through that room". Dr. Bowen says "get me a sheet, and I will cover Mr. Borden over". They started and went after that. Then when they came down I remember Mrs. Churchill saying "O, Mrs. Borden" this way. Whatever she said or did gave me that impression that she had gone too. I did not then know either of them were murdered. I supposed it was from this impression of the poison that I had had in my mind.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you first learn that they were murdered?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I got Lizzie into the dining room, on to the dining room lounge, and we were there, I dont know how long, when her Uncle came in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is Morse?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir. And something he said about their being murdered, and looked up to her, then it dawned on my mind that it was cold blooded murder. That is the first idea that I had that it was murder.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not see Morse until he got into the room where Lizzie was?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No. I had not seen Mr. Morse for years before.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That room where you were with Lizzie, was what room?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The dining room. The first room I was in was the kitchen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The sitting room was where the murdered man was?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who was with you and with Lizzie?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: In the dining room, I dont think there was any one, no one in the dining room, except when Mr. Morse came in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did you find Lizzie's appearance and condition to be, when you got there?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Dazed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I suppose she made no talk at all, excepting what you have said?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont know what she said. She said something. I says "Lizzie, dont talk".

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you found her, she was in the kitchen, you say?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She went with you then into the dining room?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long did you remain there with her?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Remain where?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: In the dining room.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I havnt any idea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where then?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont know whether I suggested, or who suggested, her going up stairs; but I know she went up stairs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go up with her?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont remember that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you up stairs?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir I was up there. I think if I did not go with her, I must have been there very soon after.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she go straight to her room, so far as you remember?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she take off any of her clothes?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont know what I was doing, but I came into the room, and found her fastening a pink wrapper on.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she change her shoes or stockings?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir, she could not have done that, I was not out of the room long enough.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you remain there with her?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Where, and how long?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long did you remain there with her in her room, after she got to her room?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont know. I think she sent me down. I dont remember, I cant tell it. I know she made this remark when she said it, I dont know, something about an undertaker. She says "If they have to have an undertaker, as I suppose they will, have Winwood."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you stay until supper time, or anything like it?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: What do you mean, in the room? I was in and out of the room all the afternoon.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She remained in the room?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, so far as I know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you finally go home, and leave the house, somewhere near supper time?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, I dont think so. I went home near six, I dont know, it seems to me it was day light, because I remember somebody stepping up to me and walking over home with me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dont mean the next morning?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No. I remember one of my old friends walking down with me, and my excusing myself and going up stairs; it must have been before dark.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had Emma got there before you went home?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: O, yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who did you leave Miss Lizzie with?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Who do you mean in the house?

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She remained in her room, as I understand you?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir. I dont know who I left her with, but I think Mrs. Holmes and Emma.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Mrs. Charles J. Holmes?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir, they were there. We intended, some of us, not to leave Lizzie; we knew the state she was in. When one was out, the other made a point to be there; which was which, I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: At any time did you have any talk with the servant girl, that you recollect?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont remember of ever saying a word to her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Or of hearing her say anything?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir, I did not hear Maggie talk much, and I have not at any other time.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear Mr. Morse say anything about it at any time that day?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont remember of anything; there might have been general talk; I dont remember of anything.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember of anything that Lizzie said about it, that remains in your memory?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, I have not asked her but one question all through it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Will you tell me what that is?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir, I asked her what she went to the barn to do. She says my screen and window. She gave me to understand they did not come together right, or something, "I was ironing handkerchiefs, and my flat iron was not hot, and I thought I would go and get that while I was waiting."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did she say she went to get?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: A piece of tin or iron to fix the screen. I found the handkerchiefs part ironed, and part damp. I took the damp ones and shook them out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you find the ironing board?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont remember seeing it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A little ironing board?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont remember it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did she tell you of the piece of tin for the screen?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The first day; I remember asking her that question, and her answering me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I suppose you had heard from somebody that she was at the barn?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember who you heard that from?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think I heard her say it. I think somebody asked her in the kitchen before we went out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: And she said she was in the barn?

speaker: Alice Russell
