testimony: That she went to the barn.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: A piece of tin for the screen for which window?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: A piece of tin or iron. She says "you know there is everything up there, and I went to see if I could not get a piece of tin or iron to fix it. The screen or the window, I dont know which she was going to fix.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you recollect anything else that she said?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I cant unless something was asked me; it might come to me then; I cant, as I am trying to tell you.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I do not like to ask this question, but I feel obliged to. Did you see enough to notice what the relations were between Miss Lizzie and her mother?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: In all my acquaintance, which is ten years sure, and most of that time has been, part of the time quite intimate, I never yet heard any wrangling in the family. I have got to answer the question, and I will say I dont think they were congenial.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What gave you the impression they were not congenial?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Because their tastes differed in every way; one liked one thing, and the other liked another.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were they together very much?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont think they were very much.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I suppose what you say about Lizzie is also true of Emma?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: About the same; it was not always the same, but it would be hard work to tell.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I judge by your saying they had a sitting room up stairs.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: They sat up there a great deal.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Their step mother did not sit up there with them?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever hear Lizzie speak of any trouble she had had with her mother?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, I suppose I have. I have heard her say that Mrs. Borden thought so and so; the same as any family.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she express to you ever that she regarded her mother as untruthful or deceitful?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont think she ever did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she ever allude particularly to any trouble she ever had with her mother?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she ever tell you what the trouble was?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Nothing further than she was a step mother. The whole thing was as far as I could see, that an own mother might have had more influence over the father; it was the father more than the mother.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What do you mean?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The father was the head of the house; they had to do as he thought. Mrs. Borden did not control the house; the whole summing up of it, was that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were her relations with her father cordial?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: So far as I know. I never saw anything different.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were they congenial?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I should not suppose they would be - knowing their different natures.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The different nature of the father and mother and Lizzie?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, each of them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was the difference in their natures?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Mr. Borden was a plain living man with ridgid ideas, and very set. They were young girls. He had earned his money, and he did not care for the things that young women in their position naturally would; and he looked upon those things. I dont know just how to put it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He did not appreciate girls?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, I dont think he did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Their ideas were more modern than his with regard to the way of living, do you mean?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did you get this, from the girls talk, or what you observed?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: From what I observed. Everybody knew what Andrew Borden's ideas were. He was a very plain living man; he did not care for anything different. It always seemed to me as if he did not see why they should care for anything different.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did they complain about it?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, they used to think it ought to be different; there was no reason why it should not be. They used to think it might be different.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Lizzie or Emma, or both?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Both.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: There never was any wrangling between them?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, I never heard any. They had quite refined ideas, and they would like to have been cultured girls, and would like to have had different advantages, and it would natural for girls to express themselves that way. I think it would have been very unnatural if they had not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: He did not give them the advantages of education that they thought they ought to have had?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont know as it is just that; but people cannot go and do and have, unless they have ample means to do it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Which stair way did she go up when she went to her room?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The front stair way.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember of Lizzie going into the room where her father lay dead, after you got there?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir. We had to pass through; we passed through this way.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you go into the hall?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: We had to, and we had to go through the sitting room; and we went right so. That is all she was in the room, I am positive of, until he was removed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Passed through the corner?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she go into the room where her mother was?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: This is an inquiry in which every person is interested, to get at the bottom facts; is there any other I have not asked you about, which you know which is material to the question, that you have not stated? Can you tell me anythingelse concerning this matter that you have not already done? It is as much your duty to tell, as it is mine to ask.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Well, I am in a much better condition to tell it than I have been.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is one reason why I postponed it as long as I could. Is there any other fact that you can tell me that you have not told me?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The morning of the funeral I went out to do some errands; and when I came back my hair was tumbled, and I took my dress waist off, and combed my hair. When I had gotten through I put my waist on again, and had nearly finished it, and I turned, and I saw something in under the bed that frightened me almost to pieces.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were sleeping in the house?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That big stick?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It is the one you gave to the marshal, the round whittled stick?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you been sleeping in the house every night?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You slept there that night?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir, that is what frightened me so much, it was in my room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was the room Mr. and Mrs. Borden occupied?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir. I occupied that when I was there.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you went into the daughter's room, did you have to go down stairs and come up?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: You dont have to if the other side was unlocked.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After the tragedy was it unlocked so you could go through?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was open then?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After the tragedy the door was unlocked?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So when you wanted to go to Lizzie or Emma's, you went in through?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you first see that stick?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think between nine and ten. I dont think I could have been gone longer than that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was not while you were at the funeral?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir. When I came back, my clothes were there, my dress was there, I went into this room I had occupied to change my dress, and turned, when I saw it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was it exactly?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: At the head of the bed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Under?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much in sight?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: So that I saw it as I turned.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had it been there before?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I had not seen it before.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you done, what they say women do?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, I had not done that.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had not looked under the bed?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So it may have been under the bed all the time?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes. I think in my frightened condition, as I look at it now, it might have been there. Then I was terribly alarmed, because I felt as if in some way it implicated me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: About as much as it implicates me, just about.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, as I look at it now.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When you saw it, it was plainly visible?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, I saw the end of it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much was it out? Indicate by your fan.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: It was not out from under the bed at all. I could see a little ways under the bed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It had no flap hanging down, a modern french bedstead?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: It was not a french bedstead; it had no varlance.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You would have been likely to have seen it before that if it had been in the same place?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I thought so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you every find out what it was?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I think it was something that her father had kept in the house.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who told you that?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I told it to Detective Handscomb; and he asked Emma. I dont think the girl knew anything about that I found it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is there any other fact that has to do with this matter that you can tell us, that you think of? My inquiry is not directed to, or at, or against anybody, or in favor of anybody.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont know of anything.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What sort of a dress was it Miss Lizzie had on before she changed it?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I dont know. I have not any idea.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You must have been badly flustrated.

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Perhaps it does seem so to you. I can tell you, any other time I have been out with the girls, sometimes a whole season, and I could not tell you what kind of hats they wore. That was not as strange as it would seem to some people. I do not observe, and I care very little about such things.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You know she changed her dress and put on a pink wrapper?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Whether the dress she had on before that was dark or light?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I havnt any idea, I cant recollect a thing.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you remember whether the dress she had on was all one piece, or a two part dress?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, I cant.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Different skirt and a different waist?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, I cant.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You spent the night there?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where did you sleep that night?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: The first two nights I slept in what was Mr. and Mrs. Borden's room; the next two nights I slept in what was Emma's room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: After you found that stick, you changed?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No, that did not make me change.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did change after that?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes. Saturday I found the stick.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you have occasion to go down stairs with Miss Lizzie that night?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The first night?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: For some purpose connected with her sickness?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: It was just wash water, water we had been using for bathing that afternoon. She started to get it out of the wash bowls &c. I said "I will go down with that". She says "I will go, if you will go and hold the lamp." I went down with her, and she emptied it. Then she went into the laundry and rinsed out the pail. I stood at the door and held the lamp.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was this?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Down cellar.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time of night was that? Was it in the night?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: O, no, before we had gone to bed.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did you get down stairs that time, do you remember?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, we came through the sitting room then, because the bodies had been put into the dining room.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The bodies had been moved then?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It was before you had gone to bed?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You were both dressed?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes Sir, so far as I can remember. I was any way, and I think she was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know what become of the dress that she did take off?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: I supposed that she hung it up in the wardrobe.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You did not notice?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: No Sir. When I came up, her dress was fastened, and she was tying the ribbon.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She had already changed the dress?

speaker: Alice Russell
testimony: Yes, they kept coming and interviewing, and I suppose she wanted to get into a respectable appearance.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your name?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Sarah B. Whitehead.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Miss or Mrs.?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Mrs.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where do you live

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: 45 Fourth street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you there the day of the tragedy, the day of the murder?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: No Sir, I was out of town.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where were you that day?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I went to Rocky Point.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Are you connected, or related in any way to Mr. or Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Mrs. Borden was my half sister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The same father, or the same mother?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: The same father.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What was your father's name?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Oliver Gray.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Her mother, I take it, was the first wife, and yours, the second?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were there other sisters or half sisters?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: There is one half sister to me, and own sister to Mrs. Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is her name?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Priscilla Fish; she lives in Hartford.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you well acquainted with the daughters, Emma and Lizzie?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Well, yes I was well acquainted with them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you on congenial terms with them?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Well, I dont know as I was. I never thought they liked me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Not on particularly friendly terms then?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: No. I always thought they felt above me.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know anything about the relations between Miss Lizzie and your half sister, Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I dont, for I never went there, very seldom, on account of those girls.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you not on good terms with your half sister?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Very good, more so, than anybody in the world.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I did not know but you would go there to see her?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I did occasionally.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she come to your house?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Yes Sir, she came very often.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she seem to be on good terms with her daughters?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: She never used to say but very little about them; she was a woman that kept everything to herself.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was there some little friction about property, that you knew about?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Not that I know of.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did not Mr. Borden buy out somebody, buy you out?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: He bought out my mother's share of the estate.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What did he do with it?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: He gave it to my sister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is, Mrs. Borden, his wife?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Yes Sir, I dont know whether he bought it, or she bought it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did your sister have property of her own, some?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I dont know how much she had.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She had some?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I dont know whether she had other personal property or not. I suppose she did, but I dont know.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: This that was bought out, and given to Mrs. Borden, was your mother's interest?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is it in, real estate or personal property'

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: It is real estate.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where is it?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: It is the house I live in.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is the value of it, about?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Well, I think when it was sold, it brought $3000.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It already belonged to the three of you?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: No, my father left half to me, and half to my mother; and my mother sold her part to my sister.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Your sister, Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So it then belonged to your sister and you?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Yes Sir. She bought it to keep in in the family.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: It now stands so, that is, to the estate of your sister, and you? You dont know what was paid for your mother's half?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I think she paid $1500.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You say she paid it, you dont know whether she paid it or Mr. Borden?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I dont know whether it was her money or his money.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Someone's money paid for it?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The deed was taken right from your mother to Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I suppose it was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long ago was that?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: It dont seem more than four or five years ago. I know my father has been dead 14 years. It dont seem to me my mother kept that property long in her hands. It was as long as four or five years ago.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is there any other fact about this matter that you can enlighten us about?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: No, there is not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dont know anything about it at all?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: No, I dont know much more than a stranger would.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know anything about whether your sister left a will?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: I do not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she ever speak about a will?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: Never.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So far as you know she did not leave any?

speaker: Sarah Whitehead
testimony: She never told me that she made one. I dont know anything about it.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your name?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Hannah H. Gifford.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where do you live?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: 39 Franklin street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you acquainted in the family of Mr. Borden?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Yes Sir I was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Ever go there?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: No Sir, never.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your business?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Cloak making.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Ladies cloaks, I suppose?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever make cloaks for either of that family?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: All three of them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Mrs. Borden, Lizzie and Emma?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was the last time you made any garment for Mrs. Borden?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: It must have been as much as two or three years ago.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When last for Emma?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: This Spring, not made one for her, but I fixed one for her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When for Lizzie?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: I made one for her also this Spring, very early this Spring.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Of course that made them come to your house more or less?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Yes, of course.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you ever hear either Miss Emma or Miss Lizzie say anything about their mother?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: I never heard Emma, but I have heard Lizzie.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What have you heard Lizzie say?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Well, she called her mother "a mean old thing."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was that?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: That was this Spring when I was doing the last work for them.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How came she to say that?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: It was some remark I made about her mother's garment, what would be becoming for her. You know Mrs. Borden was very fleshy; I spoke to her of what I thought would be becoming to Mrs. Borden. She says "well she is a mean old thing". I says "O, you dont say that Lizzie?" She says "yes, and we dont have anything to do with her, only what we are obliged to", she says.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She said that?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: She said that, yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Anything more?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Well, she says "we stay up stairs most of the time; we stay in our room most of the time." I says "you do, dont you go to your meals?" "Yes, we go to our meals, but we dont always eat with the family, with them; sometimes we wait until they are through", she says.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she tell you why?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: No. That is all she said. I did not say anything more. I was awfully surprised to hear her.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You never heard Mrs. Borden say anything, I suppose?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: No, I never heard any of them say anything against each other.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Excepting that?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: That is the only time I ever heard Lizzie either, and I was very much surprised.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she seem to be joking about it, or speaking with some feeling?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: No, she seemed to have a little feeling about it; that was all. There was no joking about it at all.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That was this last Spring?

speaker: Hannah Gifford
testimony: Yes, it was early in the Spring that I done their work.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your full name?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Eli Bence.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Mr. Bence, where is your place of business?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I work for D. R. Smith, the corner of So. Main street and Columbia, a drug store.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long has that store been there?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I could not say just positively. I should judge close on to ten years. I should think more than that. I dont know positively.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you worked there during that time?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I worked there three years last Spring, the 8th of April.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You remember the day of this murder?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I do positively.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I suppose you heard of it very soon afterwards?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was your attention then attracted to the fact of anybody's endeavoring to buy poison at your store the day before?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: My attention was directed to that point along towards six or seven o'clock in the evening of the day of the murder.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What took place, what happened, about the purchasing of the poison?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: This party came in there, and inquired if I kept prussic acid. They came in, and the second clerk went towards them to wait on them. I was standing out there, I walked in ahead. She asked me if we kept prussic acid. I informed her that we did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What happened then?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: She asked me if she could buy ten cents worth of me. I informed her we did not sell prussic acid, unless by a physician's prescription. She then said that she had bought this several times, I think; I think she said several times before. I says "well my good lady, it is something we dont sell unless by a prescription from the doctor, as it is a very dangerous thing to handle." She then walked around, and went out, turned right around.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did she say what she wanted it for?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I understood her to say she wanted it to put on the edge of a seal skin cape, if I remember rightly.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: She did not buy anything, no drug at all, no medicine?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you then know who it was?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I knew her as a Miss Borden; I have known her for sometime as a Miss Borden, but not as Andrew J. Borden's daughter until that morning. One of the gentlemen who was sitting there, when she turned around and went out, he says "that is Andrew J. Borden's daughter". I looked at her a second time then more closely than when I was talking to her. I should say it was Miss Borden.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What time of day was this?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I should say between ten and half past eleven, somewhere. I could not positively state the hour, as I was quite busy. I had been away on a vacation, and just came back that morning. I had lots of work in the back shop, I had been quite busy. But it was before I go to dinner, which is half past eleven, and I think between ten and half past eleven, positively.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you ever seen her in the store before?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: No Sir, not in that store.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You had merely seen her on the street?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Was that the day before the murder?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir, that was August third.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You said your attention was attracted to it six or seven o'clock in the evening; what attracted your attention then?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: There was a lady came in the shop; she says to me, talking of it as everybody did that day, she says, "why, I understand they are suspecting Miss Borden, the daughter."

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: This was the evening after the murder?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I do not care for that. I thought it was the evening before the murder?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You may finish it, now you have begun.

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I says "is that so?" She says yes, and walked out then. It started me to thinking of this that had happened the day before. The other gentlemanthat was there at the time, he came in shortly afterwards, and he remarked to me that that was rather a singular coincidence. I said yes I thought it was. I then spoke to one of my intimate friends, a Doctor, and I told him what I had heard &c, and he says then you ought to report it. I says I think so myself. We were busy with prescriptions at the time, I was alone at the time in the shop. About somewhere around eight o'clock Officer Doherty and Officer Harrington came up.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you afterwards go to the house?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I did, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When was that?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I should say between eight and nine o'clock.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What day was that?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: That was the fourth, the day of the murder.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: When did you go to the house?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Eight o'clock in the evening after the murder.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That same evening?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you there see Lizzie Borden?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I did, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where was she when you saw her?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: In the kitchen, talking with Officer Harrington.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you recognize her as the one that you had had the talk with the night before?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I did, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Positively?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I dont think I could be mistaken.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How did you judge?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I judged both from seeing her before on the street, and also by a peculiar expression around the eyes, which I noticed at the time, and noticed then.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you hear her talk?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you identify the voice?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You went in for the purpose of seeing if she was the one?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you speak to her that evening in there?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I did not.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who was present in the shop when she came in to buy the prussic acid?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: The second clerk, Mr. Hart, and Mr. Kilroy. The third clerk was in the back shop, and did not hear or see anything.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Kilroy and Hart were in the shop?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: The law requires a record to be made of sales of poison?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir. I calculate to see all people of that kind who come in the shop.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How came she to say what she wanted it for; did you ask her?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I did, yes sir; that is what we always do.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much would ten cents worth be?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I never sold ten cents worth in my life. I never sold it, only on prescription.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much is in an ounce?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I dont know as I could really tell you how much it does cost an ounce, it is so long ago since we bought any. I could not positively say.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Give me some idea what it would be worth to sell, nearer ten cents or fifty dollars an ounce?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I never sell it that way.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Suppose I should want to buy an ounce?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: I should not sell it to you.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What does it cost you an ounce?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: It is not expensive. I have forgotten just what it does cost, I think somewhere around 25 or 30 cents an ounce.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How much of a dose of prussic acid is fatal, do you know?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Yes Sir, I ought to. We dont keep prussic acid in its strength; we only keep the dilute, two per cent. One drop of strong prussic acid has proved fatal; the dilution is as high as four drops. I have given that on prescription. We never put up four drops without first inquiring of the Doctor. I would not like to give anybody five drops.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you had any talk with anybody else about the matter, except Mr. Hilliard and his officers, and the doctor?

speaker: Eli Bence
testimony: Only the doctor, that is all. Lots and lots of people have spoken to me about it. I have always referred them to Mr. Hilliard. Of course when it came out in the paper, everybody was inquiring of me if I had done this or done that.Hosea Knowlton: What is your name?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: Frank H. Kilroy.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your business?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: I am a student of medicine.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Where were you last Wednesday, a week ago yesterday?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: Wednesday morning, I called in D. R. Smith's drug store.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Do you know Miss Lizzie A. Borden?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: I have seen her. I know her by sight.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you seen her since the tragedy?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How long had you known her by sight?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: Perhaps a year.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you ever seen her in the store?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: No, I saw her on the street.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So you could identify her?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Now did you see her in that store, that morning?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What took place?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: She came in there, I was in front of the counter under the fan, talking with Mr. Bence, She went up to the counter, Mr. Hart the clerk was behind the counter, she asked Mr. Hart for prussic acid. At that time Mr. Bence left me and went behind the counter, and asked her if she had a prescription, that they could not sell prussic acid without a prescription. I dont know just what she said. I heard her mention seal skin cape, and she left the store.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: How was she dressed?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: I dont remember. I think she had a cape, or carried a cape or sack of some kind on her arm.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is all you know about it?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You have not been to see her since that time?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You feel sure that you know who she is?

speaker: Frank Kilroy
testimony: Well, I think so.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your name?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: Frederick B. Hart.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: What is your business?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: Clerk in D. R. Smith's drug store.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Were you present in the store Wednesday morning, a week ago?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I was.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see anybody coming in there, inquiring for prussic acid?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I did.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: State what you saw and heard.

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: Between ten and half past eleven o'clock Wednesday morning, a woman came in the store, and inquired for prussic acid. She said she wanted it to put on the edges of a seal skin cloak. She was refused the acid and went out.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Who waited on her?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: She was on the point of asking me, as the other clerk stepped up, and she asked him.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you know who it was?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I did not at the time, no air.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Have you since seen her?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I have not, no sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: So you dont know who it is, excepting by hear say?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I dont know, except from a picture I have seen.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Seen in the paper?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: In the Fall River Globe, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is all the way you know is by the picture. Does she resemble the picture of Miss Borden you have seen in the paper?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: She does.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: That is all you know about it?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: Yes Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Had you ever seen that person in the store before?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: No Sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Did you see the lady that was in custody, in company with the Marshal, as you went out?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: In black, yes sir.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: Is that the woman?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: That is the woman.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You are sure of it?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: That is sure.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You dont think you can be mistaken?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I dont think I can be mistaken.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: I think you said you had known her by sight before?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I never knew the woman by sight before.

speaker: Hosea Knowlton
testimony: You are sure this is the woman?

speaker: Frederick Hart
testimony: I am, sir, just.
